blocks.x: For two chapters we have worked on numbers, stretching them by multiplication and stacking them up with powers. Now we turn the other way and look inside a number, at how one whole number can sit neatly within another. The picture that carries this whole lesson is a simple one. Take a fixed pile of objects and try to arrange the whole pile into one full rectangle, every row the same length, no gaps and nothing left over. Doing that is the very same act as asking which numbers divide the pile evenly, because a side of such a rectangle is exactly a number that goes in without remainder. A side length like that is what we will call a factor. We will first hunt for these sides, watch them travel in pairs, and then turn the relationship all the way around to meet multiples, the numbers that a given number helps build.

A class is setting out exactly 24 floor cushions for a reading corner. They want one full rectangular block, every row the same length, no cushion left over and no gap anywhere. Find every number of rows that works by writing each one as rows times the matching row length. How many different row counts work? Enter that count.
hints: A number of rows works only if you can split 24 into that many equal rows with nothing left over. Try 1 row, then 2, then 3, and keep going, checking each time whether 24 divides up cleanly.
blocks.sol: A number of rows works exactly when it splits 24 into equal rows with nothing left over, which is the same as saying that number divides 24 evenly. Walk upward and write each working count beside the row length it forces. $$1\times24,\quad 2\times12,\quad 3\times8,\quad 4\times6$$ Each arrangement names two numbers at once, the rows and the row length, and their product is always 24. Reading both numbers out of every rectangle gives the full list of row counts that work. $$1,\ 2,\ 3,\ 4,\ 6,\ 8,\ 12,\ 24$$ These are precisely the numbers that divide 24 evenly. We call them the factors, or divisors, of 24, and there are $$\boxed{8}$$ of them. Quick check, the 4-by-6 rectangle holds \(4\times6=24\) cushions, exactly the pile we started with.

blocks.x: A museum is mounting 48 identical square photo tiles into one full rectangular grid on a wall, and the curator wants to know every grid shape that is possible. Find all the factors of 48 by walking upward from 1, and beside each factor you find, write the partner it summons. Then notice exactly when you are allowed to stop searching. How many factors does 48 have? Enter that count.
hints: The moment you find a small side like 3, the matching side is forced, since 3 rows must each be \(48\div3\) tiles long. So every small factor you find hands you a large one for free.

Watch the two sides of each pair close in on each other. Once the next side you would test is bigger than the partner it would pair with, every grid past that point is just one you have already seen turned on its side.
blocks.sol: Start at 1 and climb, writing each factor with the partner it forces, since \(d\) rows of length \(48\div d\) is the very same wall. $$1\times48,\quad 2\times24,\quad 3\times16,\quad 4\times12,\quad 6\times8$$ Each small side instantly summons its larger partner, so the factors come out already sorted into matched pairs whose product is 48. Now watch where to stop. After 6 we try 7, but 7 does not divide 48. The next number that divides, 8, is already standing on the right of the pair \(6\times8\). Once the side you are about to test would be larger than the partner it would summon, you have already met every rectangle, just mirrored, so the search is finished. Reading both numbers out of every pair gives all the factors. $$1,\ 2,\ 3,\ 4,\ 6,\ 8,\ 12,\ 16,\ 24,\ 48$$ There are $$\boxed{10}$$ of them. Quick check, the middle pair \(6\times8=48\), and we stopped right after 6 because 8 was already accounted for.

blocks.x: A whole number \(f\) is a factor of a whole number \(n\), also called a divisor of \(n\), when \(n\) objects arrange into a full rectangle with \(f\) along one side and nothing left over. That is the same as saying \(n\) divided by \(f\) leaves no remainder, and the same as saying \(n=f\times(\text{some whole number})\). Factors travel in pairs, since every side \(f\) of a rectangle of area \(n\) is matched by the partner side \(n\div f\) on the opposite edge, and the two multiply to \(n\). That pairing is why you never search all the way to \(n\). You only test sides up to the point where a side would meet its own partner, and below that crossing each small factor you find hands you its large partner for free, so the hunt finishes early.

A tile artist sets identical square tiles into one solid square, the same number of rows as columns, none left over. With \(36\) tiles, find every factor of \(36\) by writing the partner pairs the way you did for rectangles, \(1\times36,\ 2\times18,\ 3\times12,\ 4\times9,\ 6\times6\). Notice what is unusual about the \(6\times6\) arrangement. Then, without listing its factors, predict whether \(49\) has an odd or an even number of factors, and say why. Contrast that with \(35\).
hints: Every pair so far has used two different side lengths. Look hard at \(6\times6\). How many new factors does that arrangement actually contribute?
\(49=7\times7\) and \(35=5\times7\). In which one does a side pair with itself?
blocks.ans: 36 has nine factors, an odd count, because the 6-by-6 square self-pairs; 49 has an odd count, 35 has an even count
blocks.sol: Walk the pairs that fill a \(36\)-tile rectangle, each side handing you its partner. $$1\times36,\quad 2\times18,\quad 3\times12,\quad 4\times9,\quad 6\times6$$ The first four pairs each name two different factors, eight factors so far. The last one is different. The square \(6\times6\) uses the same number on both sides, so \(6\) is its own partner. It adds a single new factor, not a fresh pair. Counting them all, $$1,2,3,4,6,9,12,18,36,$$ gives \(\boxed{9}\) factors, an odd total. Here is the reason in one breath. Factors come two at a time, a small side and its larger partner, so they would always count up to an even number. The only way to break that is for one factor to pair with itself, \(d\times d=n\), which happens exactly when \(n\) is a perfect square. That lone self-paired factor is counted once, tipping the total to odd. So \(49=7\times7\) self-pairs at \(7\) and has an odd number of factors, while \(35\) splits only into the distinct pairs \(1\times35\) and \(5\times7\), four factors, an even count. Quick check on \(35\). Its factors are \(1,5,7,35\), and \(4\) is indeed even.

blocks.x: Almost every factor drags a different partner along, so factors usually come two at a time and the count is even. The one way to break that is for a factor to pair with itself, \(f\times f=n\), which happens exactly when \(n\) is a perfect square. So a whole number has an odd number of factors precisely when it is a perfect square, and the lone unpaired factor is the side of its square. An odd factor count is a fingerprint, and the print belongs to a square.

blocks.cap: A factor of a number is a way to fill a full rectangle, and the two side lengths form a factor pair. On the left, \(3\times4=12\) uses two different partners. On the right, the square \(6\times6=36\) uses one number on both sides, so the two partners have become the same factor. That single self-paired side, the gold corner on the diagonal, is the lone unpaired factor that gives a perfect square its odd total.

So far we have stood at one number and looked in at its sides. Step back now and read the same fact from the other number in the pair. If \(6\) is a factor of \(36\), then \(36\) is exactly where you arrive by stepping out in equal sixes, \(6, 12, 18, 24, 30, 36\). Seen from that side, \(36\) is a multiple of \(6\). Factor and multiple are one relationship seen from its two ends, a single arrow read in two directions.

A toy train starts on stone \(0\) of a long straight track and always rolls forward exactly \(7\) stones at a time, dropping a marker each time it stops, at \(0, 7, 14, 21,\) and onward. Without dividing, decide which of the stones \(28, 35, 50, 63\) the train actually lands on. For each stone it reaches, state the relationship between \(7\) and that stone twice, once using the word factor and once using the word multiple. How many of these four stones does the train land on? Enter that count.
hints: Keep adding \(7\). \(7, 14, 21, 28, 35, 42, 49, 56, 63\). Which of the four stones appear on that list?
blocks.sol: Skip forward by sevens and watch where the markers fall. $$7,\ 14,\ 21,\ 28,\ 35,\ 42,\ 49,\ 56,\ 63,\ \ldots$$ Stones \(28, 35,\) and \(63\) all appear, but \(50\) does not, since the train steps from \(49\) straight to \(56\) and skips right over it. The landings are exactly the stones reached by a whole number of \(7\)-steps. $$28=4\times7,\qquad 35=5\times7,\qquad 63=9\times7$$ Each landing reads two ways. Take \(28\). Read one direction, \(7\) is a factor of \(28\), since \(28=4\times7\). Read the other direction, \(28\) is a multiple of \(7\), since it is four \(7\)-steps out from \(0\). The same double reading holds for \(35\) and \(63\). Stone \(50\) gets neither, because \(7\) is not a factor of it and it is not a multiple of \(7\). The train lands on $$\boxed{28,\ 35,\ 63}.$$

blocks.x: A whole number \(m\) is a multiple of \(n\) when \(m\) sits some whole number of \(n\)-steps out from \(0\), that is \(m=k\times n\) for a whole number \(k\). This is the exact mirror of a factor. \(m\) is a multiple of \(n\) precisely when \(n\) is a factor of \(m\). One arrow. Read it forward and you say multiple, read it backward and you say factor.

A workshop sorts wooden rods by length, all whole numbers of centimeters. For each pair below, the first number is a rod and the second is the bar it is being compared against. Decide whether the first number is a factor of the second, a multiple of the second, both, or neither, and give a one-line reason for each. (a) \(4\) and \(12\) (b) \(15\) and \(5\) (c) \(8\) and \(8\) (d) \(6\) and \(9\)
hints: For each pair, ask two separate questions. Does the first number divide the second evenly, and is the first number itself a whole-number times the second?
\(a\) is a factor of \(b\) when \(b = a \times (\text{whole number})\). \(a\) is a multiple of \(b\) when \(a = b \times (\text{whole number})\). A pair can satisfy both, one, or neither.
Try the pair where both numbers are equal. Does \(8\) divide \(8\), and is \(8\) a whole-number times \(8\)?
accept: a factor b multiple c both d neither
blocks.sol: Each pair asks one question twice, once in each direction. The first number is a factor of the second when the second is a whole-number times the first, and the first is a multiple of the second when the first is a whole-number times the second. (a) \(4\) and \(12\). Is \(12\) a whole-number times \(4\)? Yes, \(12 = 4 \times 3\), so \(4\) is a factor of \(12\). Going the other way, \(4\) is not \(12\) times any whole number, so it is not a multiple of \(12\). (b) \(15\) and \(5\). Is \(15\) a whole-number times \(5\)? Yes, \(15 = 5 \times 3\), so \(15\) is a multiple of \(5\). It cannot be a factor of \(5\), since a factor never exceeds the number it divides. (c) \(8\) and \(8\). Here \(8 = 8 \times 1\) read both ways at once. \(8\) divides \(8\) evenly, so it is a factor, and \(8\) is one copy of \(8\), so it is a multiple. A number is always both a factor and a multiple of itself. (d) \(6\) and \(9\). Does \(6\) divide \(9\) evenly? No, \(9 \div 6\) leaves a remainder. Is \(6\) a whole-number times \(9\)? No, \(6\) is smaller than \(9\). So the answer is neither. Notice that \(4\) and \(12\) tell the same story as \(15\) and \(5\), just told from opposite ends. Whenever one number is a factor of another, that other number is automatically a multiple of the first. Factor and multiple are not two relationships, they are one relationship read in two directions.

blocks.x: Two students are handed opposite assignments about the number \(20\). Priya must write the complete list of every factor of \(20\), while Owen must write the complete list of every multiple of \(20\). Each is asked the same question, will you ever finish? Decide who finishes and who never does, and explain why. Write out the list that can be finished. Then say whether \(20\) itself lands on both lists.
hints: A factor of \(20\) is a side of a rectangle with area \(20\). Can a side ever be longer than \(20\)? Now think about multiples instead. After \(20, 40, 60\), is there ever a last one?
blocks.ans: Factors of 20 are finite (1, 2, 4, 5, 10, 20, six of them, largest 20); multiples are infinite; 20 sits on both lists
accept: factors finite, multiples infinite, 20 on both
blocks.sol: Priya finishes, Owen never does. Every factor of \(20\) is a side of a rectangle of area \(20\), and a side can never be longer than the area allows, so no factor can climb past \(20\) itself. Hunting in pairs gives \(1\times 20\), \(2\times 10\), \(4\times 5\), and the list closes. $$1,\ 2,\ 4,\ 5,\ 10,\ 20$$ That is six factors, the largest being \(20\), and Priya is done. Owen's list has no last entry. The multiples of \(20\) are \(20, 40, 60, 80, 100, \ldots\) and from any one of them he can always take one more step of \(20\) and reach a new one. There is no biggest multiple, so he never finishes. Notice \(20\) appears on both lists at once. It is its own largest factor, since \(20=1\times 20\), and it is its own smallest positive multiple, since \(20=20\times 1\). And right at the front of Priya's list sits \(1\), a factor of \(20\) because one long row of \(20\) is a perfectly good rectangle. The finishable list is $$\boxed{1,\ 2,\ 4,\ 5,\ 10,\ 20}$$ Quick check, each pairs cleanly, \(2\times 10=20\) and \(4\times 5=20\), so nothing is missing and nothing is extra.

blocks.x: Here is the great asymmetry. Every positive whole number has finitely many factors, each one at least \(1\) and at most the number itself, since a factor's partner side is a whole number no smaller than \(1\), which keeps the factor from ever climbing past the number. The one exception is \(0\), which every number divides, so \(0\) alone has no largest factor. But every whole number has infinitely many multiples, because from any multiple you can always step once more and reach a fresh one. Three quiet facts rode along with this. 1 is a factor of every number \(n\), since one long single row is always a valid rectangle and \(n=1\times n\). Every number is both a factor and a multiple of itself, the full-row case and the single-step case, since \(n=n\times 1\). And 0 is a multiple of every number, since taking zero steps of any size leaves you sitting at \(0\), that is \(0=n\times 0\), so \(0\) waits at the very front of every multiple list.

A coastal lighthouse sweeps a beam that flashes on every \(8\)th second, at second \(8\), then \(16\), then \(24\), and onward. A keeper sits down to log every flash whose second-number falls anywhere from \(100\) through \(250\), endpoints included. How many flashes does the keeper write down?
hints: The flashes happen exactly at the multiples of \(8\). Count every multiple of \(8\) up to \(250\), then peel off the ones that fall below \(100\).
To count multiples of \(8\) up to a number, see how many whole steps of \(8\) fit inside it. For \(250\), how many times does \(8\) go in?
blocks.sol: The flashes sit exactly at the multiples of \(8\), so this is a counting question about a stretch of the multiples line. Rather than list them all, count up to the top of the window and then subtract everything that falls below the bottom. Up to \(250\), the largest multiple of \(8\) that fits is \(248=31\times 8\), since \(32\times 8=256\) overshoots. So \(31\) multiples of \(8\) sit in \(1\) through \(250\). Below \(100\), the largest multiple of \(8\) is \(96=12\times 8\), so \(12\) multiples of \(8\) sit in \(1\) through \(99\). The flashes actually inside the window are what is left after removing the early ones. $$31-12=\boxed{19}$$ One caution worth naming, always check whether the endpoints are themselves multiples, since an endpoint that lands on a multiple has to be counted. Here neither \(100\) nor \(250\) is a multiple of \(8\), so no endpoint needs special care. Quick check, the first flash inside the window is \(104=13\times 8\) and the last is \(248=31\times 8\), and the flashes run \(13\times 8\) up through \(31\times 8\), which is \(31-13+1=19\) of them. The two counts agree.

blocks.x: To count the multiples of \(n\) inside a range, count how many sit from the start of the line up to the top of the range, then subtract how many sit below the bottom, and what survives are the ones inside. The number of multiples of \(n\) from \(1\) up to a value \(V\) is just how many whole \(n\)-steps fit, which is \(V\) divided by \(n\) with any remainder thrown away. That one division does double duty at the edges too, round it down for the largest multiple no greater than a bound, and round up for the smallest multiple at least as big as a bound. Through all of it, keep one eye on whether each endpoint is itself a multiple, since that decides whether it joins the count.

So far the multiples of a number have marched outward one at a time, each a single step past the last. Now picture two numbers that both already sit on the multiples-of-\(n\) trail. Add them together, or take one away from the other, and where do you land. Does the result stay on the trail, or does it wander off into the gaps between the markers? It is worth pausing to guess before you read on.

A token machine is fussy. It only ever hands out tokens in full packs of \(9\), or swallows them back in full packs of \(9\), nothing in between. A player starts at \(0\) tokens, receives \(153\), then receives \(72\) more, then returns \(18\). First, find the final count. Then argue, without ever dividing the totals, why the final count is forced to be a multiple of \(9\), no matter which pack sizes the machine had used.
hints: Every amount the machine moves is a whole number of \(9\)s. Write each of \(153\), \(72\), and \(18\) as \(9\) times something, then collect the \(9\)s.
If three amounts are \(9\times17\), \(9\times8\), and \(9\times2\), what happens when you pull the \(9\) out in front of the whole calculation?
blocks.sol: The straight count is \(153+72-18=207\). Now for the part that matters. Every amount the machine touches is a whole number of \(9\)s, so write them that way, \(153=9\times17\), \(72=9\times8\), and \(18=9\times2\). The running count is then $$9\times17+9\times8-9\times2=9\times(17+8-2)=9\times23=\boxed{207}.$$ The \(9\) was a shared factor of every piece, so it slid out in front of the whole combination and left a whole number, \(23\), behind. That is the heart of it. Adding or subtracting whole numbers of \(9\)s can only ever build another whole number of \(9\)s. In general, if two amounts are \(9a\) and \(9b\), then \(9a+9b=9(a+b)\) and \(9a-9b=9(a-b)\), still a whole number of \(9\)-steps. Picture it as the machine stacking full rows of \(9\). Two such blocks set on top of each other make one taller block, still full rows of \(9\) all the way up. Quick check, \(9\times23=207\), and \(153+72-18=207\) too.

blocks.x: A stadium turnstile shows a running count of \(240\), which is a multiple of \(12\) since \(240=12\times20\). A glitch tacks a stray \(17\) onto the count. First, explain why the new reading \(240+17\) cannot possibly be a multiple of \(12\). Then find the smallest positive number you could add next to bring the count back onto a multiple of \(12\).
hints: Suppose \(240+17\) were a multiple of \(12\). Then \(17\) would be the difference of two multiples of \(12\). What does the previous problem say such a difference has to be?
The new count is \(257\). Find the next multiple of \(12\) above it, then see how far away it sits.
blocks.sol: Suppose, just to test it, that \(240+17\) were itself a multiple of \(12\). Both \(240\) and that supposed total would then be multiples of \(12\), so their difference would be a multiple of \(12\) as well, by the closure you just found. But that difference is exactly \(17\), and \(17\) is not a multiple of \(12\). The supposition collapses, so \(240+17=257\) is not a multiple of \(12\). The same reasoning works every time. A multiple plus a non-multiple is always a non-multiple. To repair the count, find the next multiple of \(12\) above \(257\). Since \(12\times21=252\) and \(12\times22=264\), the count \(257\) sits just past \(252\), and the next marker is \(264\). So you must add $$264-257=\boxed{7}.$$ Here is the quiet idea underneath. The stray \(17\) is \(5\) past a multiple of \(12\), since \(17=12+5\). That \(5\) is all that knocked the count off the trail, so the count now sits \(5\) past a marker and needs \(12-5=7\) more to reach the next one. The remainder of the stray number alone decided the whole gap. Check, \(257+7=264=12\times22\).

blocks.x: Closure. Add or subtract two multiples of \(n\) and you land on a multiple of \(n\) again, because the shared \(n\) factors out of the whole thing, \(n\times a \pm n\times b = n\times(\text{a whole number})\). Now turn it around for the sharper half. A multiple of \(n\) plus a number that is not a multiple of \(n\) can never be a multiple of \(n\). If it somehow were, then subtracting the two multiples would force the stray number to be a multiple too, and it simply is not. So once you toss a non-multiple onto a multiple, you are stuck off the trail until you add another number carrying exactly the missing amount back.

A charity collects donations only in sealed envelopes. Envelope \(A\) holds some multiple of \(8\) dollars, and envelope \(B\) holds some multiple of \(8\) dollars, but you are not told either amount. A volunteer announces that the two envelopes together hold exactly \(250\) dollars. Show, without knowing either amount, that the announcement cannot be true. Then find the total closest to \(250\) that two such envelopes could actually hold.
hints: By closure, what kind of number is \(A+B\) forced to be, whatever \(A\) and \(B\) are?
Is \(250\) a multiple of \(8\)? Compare it with \(8\times31\) and \(8\times32\).
blocks.ans: Impossible; the nearest reachable total is 248
blocks.sol: You do not need either amount. Both envelopes hold multiples of \(8\), so by closure their sum \(A+B\) is forced to be a multiple of \(8\) as well. The whole question becomes a single fact about \(250\). Is \(250\) a multiple of \(8\)? Since \(8\times31=248\) and \(8\times32=256\), the number \(250\) lands between two neighbouring multiples of \(8\) and is not one of them. So no two such envelopes can ever sum to \(250\), and the announcement is impossible. The nearest total they could actually reach is the closer of those two neighbours, $$\boxed{248},$$ which is just \(2\) below \(250\) and is reachable, for instance with \(A=240\) and \(B=8\). Notice what closure did here. It let you prove something about a sum whose two pieces you could not even see.

blocks.x: One last piece of structure. Sometimes the multiples of one number sit right on top of the multiples of another, marker for marker. If a number is built from equal jumps of \(12\), is it automatically reachable by jumps of \(3\)? And does that pull the other way too? It feels like the answer should be yes at least one way around. The next problem settles which way, and it is exactly the rule the divisibility shortcuts of the next section will lean on.

At a candle workshop, beeswax sheets arrive bundled, and every bundle holds exactly \(12\) sheets. Since \(12=4\times3\), a bundle can be laid out as \(3\) rows of \(4\) sheets, or just as neatly as \(4\) rows of \(3\) sheets. Suppose a delivery is some whole number of full bundles, so the sheet count is a multiple of \(12\). (1) Explain why that sheet count must also be a multiple of \(3\). (2) Decide whether it must also be a multiple of \(4\). (3) Now run the arrow the other way. If the workshop has a pile whose size is a multiple of \(3\), must that pile pack into full bundles of \(12\), that is, must it be a multiple of \(12\)? Give one pile size that is a multiple of \(3\) but not a multiple of \(12\). Enter that one counterexample.
hints: For (1) and (2), picture one bundle as \(3\) rows of \(4\) (or \(4\) rows of \(3\)). If every single bundle already splits into a whole number of threes, what happens when you have several bundles stacked together?

For (3), look for a heap that you can split into groups of \(3\) with none left over, yet that is too small or too lopsided to fill whole bundles of \(12\). Try counting up \(3, 6, 9, \ldots\) and check each against \(12\).
blocks.sol: This whole problem is one idea, chaining. A multiple of a multiple is a multiple, so once \(12\) is built from \(3\) and \(4\), anything built from \(12\) inherits those smaller pieces. (1) A delivery of full bundles is some whole number of bundles, say \(n\) of them, so the sheet count is \(n\times12\). Replace each bundle by its \(3\) rows of \(4\). Every bundle becomes \(4+4+4\), a whole number of threes, so $$n\times12 = n\times(4\times3) = (n\times4)\times3,$$ which is \(3\) multiplied by a whole number. So the count is a multiple of \(3\). The threes that hid inside one bundle are still there when you stack many bundles. (2) Yes, by the same chaining, and now using the other layout. Each bundle is \(3\) rows of \(4\), so $$n\times12 = n\times(3\times4) = (n\times3)\times4,$$ which is \(4\) times a whole number. So a multiple of \(12\) is always a multiple of \(4\) too. (3) No, the arrow does not run backward. Being a multiple of \(3\) does not force a pile to be a multiple of \(12\). A pile of $$\boxed{6}$$ sheets splits cleanly into \(2\) groups of \(3\), so it is a multiple of \(3\), yet \(6\) is less than one full bundle of \(12\), so it is not a multiple of \(12\). Quick check, \(6=3\times2\) confirms the multiple of \(3\), and \(6\) sitting between \(0\) and \(12\) confirms it is not a multiple of \(12\). (The very smallest multiple of \(3\) that misses \(12\) is actually \(3\) itself, so \(6\) is just one easy witness, not the smallest.) Chaining only flows one way, from the bigger building block down to the pieces inside it, never back up.

blocks.x: Chaining. If \(a\) is a factor of \(b\), then every multiple of \(b\) is automatically a multiple of \(a\). The multiple carries \(b\) inside it, and \(b\) carries \(a\) inside it, so \(a\) is in there too. Stepping in \(b\)-sized steps is secretly stepping in \(a\)-sized steps. A multiple of \(12\), for instance, is always a multiple of \(1, 2, 3, 4, 6\) as well, every factor of \(12\) inherited for free. The chain runs only downward, toward the smaller factors, never upward, since the larger number may demand a factor the smaller one never carried. That is why a multiple of \(3\) need not be a multiple of \(12\). This one inheritance is exactly what lets the divisibility shortcuts of the next section lean on smaller factors.

A vault opens to a secret number that is known to satisfy three conditions at once. It is a multiple of \(6\), it is a perfect square, and it lies between \(100\) and \(400\) inclusive. Find every value the secret number could be, and argue that your list leaves nothing out.
hints: If a square \(k\times k\) is a multiple of \(6\), the side \(k\) itself must supply the factor \(2\) and the factor \(3\). What does that force \(k\) to be a multiple of?
So the secret number is the square of a multiple of \(6\). Which multiples of \(6\) have squares between \(100\) and \(400\)?
blocks.sol: The two conditions, perfect square and multiple of \(6\), squeeze together harder than they first look. Suppose the number is \(k\times k\) and is a multiple of \(6\). A multiple of \(6\) carries a factor of \(2\) and a factor of \(3\). But \(2\) and \(3\) cannot be conjured from nowhere. For the square \(k\times k\) to contain them, the side \(k\) must already supply a factor of \(2\) and a factor of \(3\) on its own, which makes \(k\) a multiple of \(6\). Then \(k\times k\) is a multiple of \(6\times6=36\). So the hunt collapses to squaring multiples of \(6\) and keeping the ones in range. Walking them, \(6^2=36\) is below \(100\), \(12^2=144\) and \(18^2=324\) both land between \(100\) and \(400\), and \(24^2=576\) overshoots. Nothing else can appear, since any valid number had to be the square of a multiple of \(6\) and those are the only such squares in the window. The secret number is therefore $$\boxed{144 \text{ or } 324}.$$ Check, \(144=12^2\) and \(144=6\times24\), while \(324=18^2\) and \(324=6\times54\), so both are genuine perfect squares and genuine multiples of \(6\) inside the range. The pleasing twist is that asking for a square that is also a multiple of \(6\) quietly forced a multiple of \(36\).

blocks.x: One relationship carried this whole lesson, read from its two ends. A factor of \(n\) is a side of a rectangle of area \(n\), and because every side has a partner side, factors arrive in pairs, with the single self-paired side of a perfect square as the lone exception that tips its factor count to odd. Turn the same arrow around and the small number becomes a factor while the large one becomes a multiple. From there flowed the quiet facts, a number has finitely many factors but infinitely many multiples, \(1\) divides everything, every number divides itself and is its own smallest positive multiple, and \(0\) is a multiple of every number. To count multiples inside a window, count up to the top and subtract the ones below the bottom. By closure, sums and differences of multiples of \(n\) stay multiples of \(n\), so a stray non-multiple can never be added back to reach one. And by chaining, a multiple of a multiple is a multiple, the inheritance running only toward the smaller factors. That last fact is about to earn its keep, because the divisibility tests waiting in the next section are nothing more than chaining put to work.

review.x: A school is fitting 40 identical lockers into one full rectangular block on a hallway wall, the same number in every row, none left over, and the caretaker wants to know every block shape that would work. List all the factors of 40 by climbing upward from \(1\), and beside each factor you find, write the partner it summons, since a block with \(d\) lockers along one side forces \(40\div d\) along the other. Then report how many factors there are in all. Enter the count.
hints: Climb from \(1\) and test each small number in turn. The moment one divides \(40\), the partner side is handed to you for free, since \(d\) lockers per row forces \(40\div d\) rows.

Watch the two sides of each pair close in on each other. Once the next number you would test is larger than the partner it would summon, every block past that point is one you have already seen turned on its side, so the hunt is done.
The pairs are \(1\times40,\ 2\times20,\ 4\times10,\ 5\times8\). Read both numbers out of every pair, sort them, and count how many you have.
review.sol: Climb from \(1\) and let each small factor summon its partner, since a side of \(d\) lockers forces \(40\div d\) on the opposite edge. $$1\times 40,\qquad 2\times 20,\qquad 4\times 10,\qquad 5\times 8$$ The number \(3\) is skipped since it does not divide \(40\), and so are \(6\) and \(7\). Each small side that works instantly hands you its larger partner, so the factors come out already sorted into matched pairs whose product is \(40\). Now watch where to stop. After \(5\) the next try would be \(6\), but \(6\) would summon a partner smaller than \(6\), one already standing inside the pair \(5\times 8\). Once the side you are about to test would be larger than the partner it summons, you have already met every block, just mirrored, so the search is finished. Reading both numbers out of every pair and sorting them gives $$1,\ 2,\ 4,\ 5,\ 8,\ 10,\ 20,\ 40,$$ which is \(\boxed{8}\) factors. They come in four clean pairs, no side meeting itself, so the count is even, exactly what you expect from a number that is not a perfect square. Check the middle pair, \(5\times 8 = 40\), the very pile we started with. The general idea is that factors travel in partner pairs that multiply to the number, so you only hunt up to where a side would meet its own partner, and each small factor you find drags its larger partner along for free.

review.x: In a ring-toss game at the school fair, the scoreboard only ever jumps up by \(8\) points, since every successful throw is worth exactly \(8\) and a miss adds nothing. So any score that ever appears must be a multiple of \(8\). Five friends each claim a final score, namely \(24\), \(30\), \(48\), \(56\), and \(60\). Decide which of these scores the game could actually show, with a quick reason for each. How many of the five scores are possible? Enter that count.
hints: A score is possible exactly when \(8\) divides it with nothing left over, since the board only climbs \(8\) at a time. Try dividing each score by \(8\) and watch for a remainder.
Count the multiples of \(8\) out loud, \(8, 16, 24, 32, 40, 48, 56, 64\), and check off any claimed score you land on exactly. The ones you skip over cannot appear.
review.sol: Since the board moves up \(8\) at a time and never by anything smaller, a score can show up only if it is built from a whole number of \(8\)-point throws, which is exactly what it means to be a multiple of \(8\). So I test each score by dividing by \(8\) and asking whether it comes out clean. $$24 = 8 \times 3, \quad 48 = 8 \times 6, \quad 56 = 8 \times 7$$ Those three land squarely on a multiple of \(8\), so they are scores the game really could display, reached in \(3\), \(6\), and \(7\) successful throws. The other two miss. Dividing \(30\) by \(8\) gives \(3\) with \(6\) left over, since \(8 \times 3 = 24\) and \(8 \times 4 = 32\) overshoots, so \(30\) is stranded between two multiples and can never appear. Dividing \(60\) by \(8\) gives \(7\) with \(4\) left over, since \(8 \times 7 = 56\) and \(8 \times 8 = 64\), so \(60\) is impossible too. The possible scores are $$\boxed{24,\ 48,\ 56}$$ Check the largest one, \(8 \times 7 = 56\), seven clean throws and not a point wasted. The general idea is that a number is a multiple of \(8\) exactly when \(8\) divides it with zero remainder, so testing for a multiple is just one division and a glance at what, if anything, is left over.

review.x: Here are four claims about the number \(61\). Decide whether each is true or false, and hold a one-line reason for each in your head. (a) \(1\) is a factor of \(61\). (b) \(61\) is a multiple of \(61\). (c) \(0\) is a multiple of \(61\). (d) \(61\) is a factor of \(0\). When you are sure, enter your verdict as the four words for (a), (b), (c), (d) in order, for example "true true true true".
hints: Lean on the definition. b is a factor of a exactly when \(a = b \times (\text{whole number})\), and then a is a multiple of b. Test each claim by trying to write that little equation.
For (a) ask what \(61 \div 1\) is, and for (b) ask what \(61 \div 61\) is. For (c) and (d), notice they are the very same sentence \(0 = 61 \times 0\) read from its two ends.
review.sol: The whole question turns on one definition, that \(b\) is a factor of \(a\) when \(a = b \times (\text{whole number})\), and in the same breath \(a\) is then a multiple of \(b\). So I just hunt for that equation in each claim. For (a), \(61 = 1 \times 61\), so \(1\) is a factor of \(61\), and in fact \(1\) is a factor of every whole number since multiplying by \(1\) changes nothing. True. For (b), \(61 = 61 \times 1\), so \(61\) is a multiple of itself, just as every number is, since one copy of a number is already a multiple of it. True. Now (c) and (d) are not two separate facts at all, they are the single line $$0 = 61 \times 0$$ read from its two ends. Read with \(0\) as the result, it says \(0\) is a multiple of \(61\), so (c) is True, and indeed \(0\) is a multiple of every number, because zero copies of anything still land on \(0\). Read with \(61\) as the thing doing the multiplying, the very same line says \(61\) is a factor of \(0\), so (d) is True, and every number is a factor of \(0\). All four claims hold, so the verdict is \(\boxed{\text{all four true}}\). Check the quiet equation that powers the last two, \(61 \times 0 = 0\), one sentence wearing two names. The general idea is that \(1\) divides everything, every number is a multiple of itself, and \(0\) is the universal multiple that every number divides, with "factor" and "multiple" being the same relationship seen from opposite sides.

review.x: A gardener sets exactly 56 identical tulip bulbs into one full rectangular flower bed, every row holding the same count, with no bulb missing and no gap. The two plainest rectangles sit at the far ends of the full list of options, the longest skinny strip and the single tall column. Find the smallest factor of \(56\) and the largest factor of \(56\), and give a one-line reason for each. Enter your answer as the smallest and the largest, like A and B.
accept: the smallest is 1 and the largest is 56
hints: A factor of \(56\) is any whole number that divides it evenly, so think about the two rectangles at the far ends, the single long strip and the one stacked into a single column.

One special number divides every whole number with nothing left over, which fixes the smallest factor right away. For the largest, ask whether any side length could ever be bigger than the whole bed itself.

Every factor of \(56\) travels with a partner, and the two partners multiply to \(56\). Picture the partner of the very smallest whole number that divides anything, and picture what a factor bigger than \(56\) would force its partner to be. Both ends of the list fall straight out of that partner pairing once you push each side as far as it can go.
review.sol: Every factor of \(56\) travels with a partner, the two side lengths of a rectangle, and the two numbers always multiply to \(56\). Start at the bottom end. The number \(1\) divides every whole number cleanly, since \(56=1\times56\), so \(1\) is always present and nothing smaller than \(1\) is a positive whole number, which makes \(1\) the smallest factor of any number. Now climb to the top end. Suppose some factor \(f\) were larger than \(56\) itself. Its partner would be \(56\div f\), and since \(f\) is bigger than \(56\) that partner would have to be a whole number smaller than \(1\), which simply does not exist. So no factor can exceed the number, and the largest factor is the number reaching all the way up to its own size, namely \(56\), paired with \(1\) on the other side. $$\text{smallest}=1\times56,\qquad \text{largest}=56\times1$$ The two bookends are therefore $$\boxed{1\ \text{and}\ 56}.$$ Check, \(1\times56=56\) and \(56\times1=56\), both rebuild the original bed, and the same reasoning would hand back \(1\) and the number itself for any whole number you choose. The general idea is that the factors of a number are bookended by \(1\) on the low end and the number on the high end, because \(1\) divides everything and a factor can never climb past the number it divides.

review.x: Two bookends sit on every factor list. 1 is always the smallest factor, the single full-length row, and the number itself is always the largest, since no side of a rectangle can outrun its own area. So a factor of \(n\) is always trapped between \(1\) and \(n\), which is why a factor hunt can never wander off forever.

A games club is printing three jigsaw puzzles for the holiday fair, one with \(72\) pieces, one with \(81\) pieces, and one with \(98\) pieces. For each puzzle the club wants to list every way to arrange the pieces into a full rectangle, since each rectangle uses a factor of the piece count as one side. Mei notices that for two of the puzzles the rectangles come in matching swap pairs, a wide one and a tall one for each shape, but for the third puzzle one rectangle has no partner to swap with, leaving an odd number of factors in all. Without breaking any number into primes, decide which of \(72\), \(81\), and \(98\) has an odd number of factors and explain it through self-pairing. Enter the single piece count.
hints: Every factor \(d\) of a number teams up with its partner \(n \div d\), so the factors normally march in two-by-two. A factor can lose its partner only when \(d\) and \(n \div d\) are the very same number, which happens exactly when \(n\) is a perfect square.

So the lone puzzle is whichever piece count is some whole number times itself. Notice \(72\) and \(98\) both sit between two neighboring squares, so try squaring the small whole numbers in that range and see which piece count one of them matches.
review.sol: The quick way to spot an odd factor count is to remember how factors travel, since each factor \(d\) of a number \(n\) walks arm in arm with its partner \(n \div d\), and the two multiply back to \(n\). Pair off this way and the factors normally come two at a time, which makes the total even. The only escape is a factor whose partner is itself, where \(d = n \div d\), and that forces \(d \times d = n\). So a number breaks the even pattern exactly when it is a perfect square, because then its square root stands alone with no separate partner to swap with. The whole question becomes which of \(72\), \(81\), \(98\) is a whole number times itself. Squaring the nearby whole numbers gives $$8 \times 8 = 64, \qquad 9 \times 9 = 81, \qquad 10 \times 10 = 100,$$ and only \(81\) appears, sitting right at \(9 \times 9\). For \(81\) the side \(9\) pairs with itself, the lone unpaired factor that tips the count to odd, while the rest still pair up as \(1 \times 81\) and \(3 \times 27\). The number \(72\) falls between \(64\) and \(81\) and \(98\) falls between \(81\) and \(100\), so neither is any whole number squared, which means every one of their factors finds a distinct partner and their counts stay even. The puzzle with an odd number of factors is \(\boxed{81}\). Check that \(81\) really is special by listing its factors, \(1, 3, 9, 27, 81\), which is five of them, an odd count, with \(9\) standing in the middle as its own partner. The general idea is that an odd factor count is the fingerprint of a perfect square, since only a perfect square owns a factor that pairs with itself.

review.x: A long ribbon is marked off in equal segments of \(23\) centimeters each, and a tailor wants to cut it at the mark that is as far along as possible while still staying under \(500\) centimeters. Each cut mark sits at a multiple of \(23\). At what mark, in centimeters, should the tailor cut so that the cut is the largest multiple of \(23\) below \(500\)? Enter that single number.
hints: The cut marks land on \(23, 46, 69,\) and so on, the multiples of \(23\). The biggest one under \(500\) is just as many whole \(23\)-steps as fit beneath the cap.
Divide \(500\) by \(23\) and throw away the remainder. That whole number of steps, times \(23\), is the mark you want.
Since \(500 \div 23\) is \(21\) with something left over, take \(21\) full steps and compute \(21 \times 23\).
review.sol: The largest multiple of \(23\) below a cap is found by fitting as many whole \(23\)-steps under the cap as I can, so I divide and round down. $$500 \div 23 = 21 \text{ remainder } 17$$ The remainder \(17\) tells me \(500\) is not itself a multiple of \(23\), and that exactly \(21\) full steps fit beneath it. Those \(21\) steps land the cut at $$21 \times 23 = 483.$$ The very next mark would be \(22 \times 23 = 506\), which sails past \(500\), so there is no multiple of \(23\) hiding between \(483\) and \(500\). The tailor cuts at \(\boxed{483}\) centimeters. Check, \(483\) is under \(500\) and \(483 + 23 = 506\) is over, so \(483\) really is the last mark before the cap. The general idea is that the greatest multiple of \(n\) below a bound is the bound divided by \(n\) with the remainder dropped, then multiplied back by \(n\).

review.x: A bakery packs its morning rolls into trays that each hold exactly 26 rolls, no more and no less, so the number baked is always a multiple of 26. The head baker wants to put out a sign saying "over 100 fresh rolls today," so she needs to bake the smallest number of full trays that still reaches at least three digits. What is that smallest three-digit multiple of 26? Enter the single number she should aim for.
hints: A three-digit number is anything from 100 up to 999, so you want the first multiple of 26 that lands at or above 100.
Instead of guessing, divide 100 by 26 to see how many trays it takes to get near the bound, then round that count up to a whole number of trays.
\(100 \div 26 \approx 3.8\), and you cannot bake a fraction of a tray, so round up to 4 trays and compute \(26 \times 4\).
review.sol: Climb the multiples of 26 one tray at a time and watch for the moment the total first reaches three digits. $$26\times 1 = 26,\qquad 26\times 2 = 52,\qquad 26\times 3 = 78,\qquad 26\times 4 = 104$$ Three trays give \(78\), which is still two digits, so it falls short of the \(100\) mark. The very next tray jumps to \(104\), the first total that reaches three digits, so the answer is \(\boxed{104}\). Here is the quicker way to see it without listing, which is the real idea. A three-digit number is anything from \(100\) upward, so I ask how many trays it takes to reach \(100\) by dividing, \(100 \div 26 \approx 3.8\). Since I cannot bake \(3.8\) trays, I round that count up to the next whole tray, \(4\), and then \(26 \times 4 = 104\). Rounding up is the key move, because rounding down to \(3\) would land below the bound at \(78\). Check the answer is genuinely a multiple of 26 by confirming \(26 \times 4 = 104\), and that \(104 \div 26 = 4\) with nothing left over. The general idea is that the smallest multiple of \(n\) at or above a bound \(B\) comes from dividing \(B\) by \(n\) and rounding the quotient up to a whole number, then multiplying back by \(n\).

review.x: At the harbor festival, Tomas hangs a row of glass floats and tags each one with a two-digit code. A float gets lit from inside only when its code is a two-digit multiple of \(6\) whose two digits add up to \(6\). Instead of scanning a long list of multiples, build the codes straight from the digit rule, find every code that lights up, and explain why your handful is the whole set. How many codes light up? Enter that count.
hints: Start from the digit rule rather than the multiples. A two-digit code has a tens digit and a units digit, and you need those two digits to add to \(6\). Write down every pair of digits that sums to \(6\) first, then worry about the multiple of \(6\) part.

The tens digit can run from \(1\) to \(6\), and once you pick it the units digit is forced so the two add to \(6\). That gives the codes \(15, 24, 33, 42, 51, 60\). Now you only need to decide which of these six are multiples of \(6\).

A multiple of \(6\) is just a number that is both even and a multiple of \(3\). Every code on your short list already has digit sum \(6\), so each one is automatically a multiple of \(3\). That leaves a single test, which of \(15, 24, 33, 42, 51, 60\) are even.
review.sol: The slick move is to build the codes from the digit rule first, since the condition that the two digits add to \(6\) is far more restrictive than the multiple of \(6\) condition and leaves only a tiny list to check. A two-digit code has a tens digit and a units digit, and the tens digit cannot be \(0\), so let the tens digit run from \(1\) up to \(6\). Each choice forces the units digit, because the two digits must total \(6\), which gives the pairs \(1\) and \(5\), then \(2\) and \(4\), then \(3\) and \(3\), then \(4\) and \(2\), then \(5\) and \(1\), then \(6\) and \(0\). A tens digit of \(7\) or more is impossible since even a units digit of \(0\) would already push the sum past \(6\), so the complete list of two-digit numbers with digit sum \(6\) is $$15,\ 24,\ 33,\ 42,\ 51,\ 60,$$ exactly six of them and no more. Now bring in the multiple of \(6\) requirement, and here is the shortcut that makes the whole thing easy. A number is a multiple of \(6\) precisely when it is both even and a multiple of \(3\), and every code on this short list has digit sum \(6\), which is itself a multiple of \(3\), so every one of these six is already divisible by \(3\) for free. The only gate left is being even, so I just keep the codes that end in an even digit, which are \(24\), \(42\), and \(60\), while \(15\), \(33\), and \(51\) end in odd digits and fall away. The lit floats therefore carry the codes $$\boxed{24,\ 42,\ 60}.$$ Check each one, \(24=6\times 4\) with \(2+4=6\), \(42=6\times 7\) with \(4+2=6\), and \(60=6\times 10\) with \(6+0=6\), all three passing both rules, and since I built the candidate list from every possible digit pair that sums to \(6\), nothing was skipped and no fourth code can exist. The general idea is that when two rules overlap, you start from whichever rule produces the shorter list, and a digit sum being fixed at \(6\) handily settles the divisibility by \(3\) on its own, so the search collapses to a single even or odd check.

review.x: A planetarium runs its evening show on the hour, and the lobby clock above the dome blinks a three-digit "shows remaining this season" counter that the staff want to leave on a striking number for opening night. They have decided the number must be a multiple of 15, and to make it look sharp on the display every one of its three digits has to be different from the other two. They want the largest such number that will fit in three digits. Enter that single number.
hints: A number is a multiple of 15 exactly when it is a multiple of both 3 and 5, so it has to end in 0 or 5. That alone narrows the search a lot.
Start at the very top three-digit multiple of 15 and step downward by 15, checking each one for a repeated digit.
The top multiple is 990, but its two 9s break the all-different rule. Keep dropping by 15 and look at the next candidate.
review.sol: Since \(15 = 3 \times 5\), a multiple of 15 must be divisible by both 3 and 5, so it has to end in \(0\) or \(5\). The largest three-digit multiple of 15 sits right below 1000, and \(1000 \div 15\) is not a whole number, so I climb down to the nearest one. $$990 = 15 \times 66$$ That is the greatest three-digit multiple of 15, but its digits are \(9, 9, 0\), and the two 9s repeat, so it fails the all-different rule. Stepping down by 15 lands me on the next candidate. $$975 = 15 \times 65$$ Its digits are \(9, 7, 5\), all three different from one another, so this one qualifies. Because I am walking downward from the very top and 975 is the first survivor I meet, nothing larger can work, and the answer is \(\boxed{975}\). Check that 975 really is a multiple of 15 by testing both factors, its digit sum is \(9 + 7 + 5 = 21\) which is divisible by 3, and it ends in 5 so it is divisible by 5, so it is divisible by 15. The general idea is that you can hunt for the best number meeting a rule by searching from the extreme end and walking inward, stopping the instant the extra constraint, here all digits distinct, is finally satisfied.

review.x: A small open-air amphitheater has \(7\) curved rows of seats, and the rows get wider as you climb. The front row seats \(14\) people, the next holds \(28\), the one behind it holds \(42\), and the pattern keeps going so that each row holds \(14\) more seats than the row just in front of it, all the way up to the highest row. Find the total number of seats in the whole amphitheater. Enter that single total.
hints: Each row count is \(14\) times its position from the front, so row one holds \(14\times 1\), row two holds \(14\times 2\), and so on up to row seven.
Once every row is written as \(14\) times something, you can pull the \(14\) out front and only add \(1+2+3+4+5+6+7\).
Add \(1\) through \(7\) by pairing \(1\) with \(7\), \(2\) with \(6\), \(3\) with \(5\), three pairs of \(8\) with \(4\) left in the middle, which gives \(28\). Then multiply by \(14\).
review.sol: Start by writing down what sits in each row as you climb. The front row has \(14\), the next has \(28\), then \(42\), and since every row adds another \(14\), the seven counts are \(14, 28, 42, 56, 70, 84, 98\). The slow way is to add those seven biggish numbers straight across, but there is a much friendlier move waiting in plain sight, since every one of them is \(14\) times its row position counting from the front, $$14 = 14\times 1,\quad 28 = 14\times 2,\quad 42 = 14\times 3,\quad \dots,\quad 98 = 14\times 7.$$ Because the same factor of \(14\) rides along with every term, I can pull it out front and rewrite the whole total as \(14\times(1+2+3+4+5+6+7)\). Now the hard part has shrunk to adding the plain counting numbers \(1\) through \(7\), and the pairing trick finishes that in a breath, since \(1+7=8\), \(2+6=8\), and \(3+5=8\), which is three pairs of \(8\) with the lonely \(4\) sitting in the middle, so \(3\times 8 + 4 = 28\). That leaves a single clean multiplication, $$14\times 28 = \boxed{392}.$$ Check by grouping the original row counts the same friendly way, \(14+98 = 112\), \(28+84 = 112\), \(42+70 = 112\), three pairs of \(112\) plus the middle row of \(56\), and \(3\times 112 + 56 = 336 + 56 = 392\), which matches. The general idea is that when a list of quantities all share a common step, you can factor that step out front and only sum the small counting numbers that remain, often pairing them from the ends to land the answer in your head.

review.x: A long-distance runner trains on a perfectly straight track, and out of habit she claps once at every marker post, the posts standing at metre \(13\), then \(26\), then \(39\), and onward at every \(13\)th metre. Her coach watches only the stretch of track that runs from metre \(150\) up to metre \(850\), and he wants to know how many claps happen with both feet strictly inside that stretch, counting no clap that falls exactly at \(150\) or exactly at \(850\). How many claps does the coach count? Enter the single number.
hints: The claps land exactly at the multiples of \(13\). Instead of listing them, count every multiple of \(13\) up to the top of the stretch, then peel off the ones that sit below the bottom.
To count multiples of \(13\) below a bound, see how many whole \(13\)-steps fit inside it. How many times does \(13\) go into \(849\), and how many times into \(150\)?

First multiple of \(13\) above \(150\) is \(13\times 12 = 156\), since \(13\times 11 = 143\) is too low. Last one below \(850\) is \(13\times 65 = 845\), since \(13\times 66 = 858\) overshoots. Now count from the \(12\)th marker through the \(65\)th.
review.sol: The claps sit exactly at the multiples of \(13\), so this is a counting question about a stretch of the multiples line. Rather than list every post, count up to the top of the window and then subtract everything that falls below the bottom. For the top, the largest multiple of \(13\) that stays strictly under \(850\) is \(845 = 13\times 65\), since \(13\times 66 = 858\) sails past \(850\). So \(65\) multiples of \(13\) sit from the start of the line up through \(845\). For the bottom, the largest multiple of \(13\) that stays at or below \(150\) is \(143 = 13\times 11\), since \(13\times 12 = 156\) already pokes above \(150\). So \(11\) multiples of \(13\) sit below the window. The claps actually inside the stretch are what is left after removing those early ones. $$65 - 11 = \boxed{54}$$ One caution always worth naming, check whether either endpoint is itself a multiple, since an endpoint landing on a marker would have to be counted. Here \(150 = 13\times 11 + 7\) and \(850 = 13\times 65 + 5\), so neither \(150\) nor \(850\) is a multiple of \(13\), and the word strictly costs us nothing. Quick check, the first clap inside the window is \(156 = 13\times 12\) and the last is \(845 = 13\times 65\), and the markers run from the \(12\)th through the \(65\)th, which is \(65 - 12 + 1 = 54\) of them. Both counts agree. The general idea is that to count the multiples of \(n\) inside a range you count how many sit up to the top and subtract how many sit below the bottom, keeping one eye on whether each endpoint is itself a multiple.

review.x: Two orchard delivery trucks pull up to the packing yard, and each truck carries some number of apples, packed only in full crates of \(15\) apples so that every truck holds a multiple of \(15\) apples. Nobody has unloaded the trucks, so the exact amounts are unknown. A dispatcher insists the two trucks together carry exactly \(400\) apples. First show, using closure, that \(400\) is impossible for any two such trucks. Then find the total nearest to \(400\) that two trucks like these could actually carry, and enter that single number.
hints: Each truck holds a multiple of \(15\). What can you say about the sum of two multiples of \(15\)? Then ask whether \(400\) is itself a multiple of \(15\).

Closure says the sum of two multiples of \(15\) is again a multiple of \(15\). Since \(15\times 26=390\) and \(15\times 27=405\), the number \(400\) sits strictly between two neighboring multiples, so it is not one of them.

The only totals possible are multiples of \(15\). Compare the two multiples on either side of \(400\), namely \(390\) and \(405\), and see which is closer, then check that the closer one is actually reachable as a sum of two multiples of \(15\).
review.sol: Write each truck as a multiple of \(15\), say \(15a\) apples on one and \(15b\) on the other, where \(a\) and \(b\) are whole numbers. Their total factors a \(15\) right out front. $$15a+15b=15(a+b)$$ Since \(a+b\) is again a whole number, the total is itself a multiple of \(15\). This is closure, adding two multiples of \(15\) can never escape the multiples of \(15\). So the only question is whether \(400\) is one of them. Walk the multiples of \(15\) near \(400\). $$15\times 26=390,\qquad 15\times 27=405$$ The number \(400\) lands strictly between these two neighbors, with no multiple of \(15\) in the gap, so \(400\) is not a multiple of \(15\) and the dispatcher's claim is impossible. Now hunt for the reachable total nearest \(400\). The candidates are exactly its two neighbors, \(390\) and \(405\). Measure each gap. $$400-390=10,\qquad 405-400=5$$ So \(405\) wins, sitting only \(5\) away while \(390\) is twice as far. It remains to confirm \(405\) is genuinely reachable as a sum of two trucks, each a multiple of \(15\). It is, for instance one truck with a single crate and the other with \(26\) crates. $$15+390=405$$ So the total cannot be \(400\), and the nearest total two such trucks could actually carry is \(\boxed{405}\). Check, \(405=15\times 27\), a clean multiple of \(15\), and \(15+390\) builds it from two valid trucks. The general idea is that a closed set, here the multiples of \(15\), can prove a target is out of reach the moment the target fails the membership test, and the best you can do is the nearest member to it.

review.x: A bead artist at a craft fair only ever ties off her bracelets in full kits of 24 glass beads, so whatever the day's bead count turns out to be, it is guaranteed to be a multiple of \(24\). A curious visitor wants to know which other group sizes that count is forced to divide evenly into. For each of \(2,\ 3,\ 4,\ 8,\ 9,\) and \(16\), decide whether the bead count must be a multiple of that number. How many of the six sizes are guaranteed? Enter that count.
hints: A multiple of \(24\) can be written as \(24 \times k\) for some whole number \(k\). First list everything that divides \(24\) itself, since each of those factors rides along inside every copy of \(24\).

If \(d\) is a factor of \(24\), then \(24 = d \times m\), so \(24 \times k = d \times (mk)\), which means \(d\) divides the count too. Check whether \(9\) and \(16\) are actually factors of \(24\) before you trust them.
For any size that is not a factor of \(24\), the number \(24\) itself is the cleanest test case, since \(24\) is certainly a multiple of \(24\). Does \(9\) divide \(24\)? Does \(16\) divide \(24\)?
review.sol: The whole game is to see which numbers are baked into \(24\), because a multiple of \(24\) carries along every factor that \(24\) already has, and nothing more is promised. Start by laying out the factors of \(24\) in pairs, $$1\times 24,\qquad 2\times 12,\qquad 3\times 8,\qquad 4\times 6,$$ so the factors of \(24\) are \(1,\ 2,\ 3,\ 4,\ 6,\ 8,\ 12,\ 24\). Here is the chaining idea that does all the work. The bead count is some \(24\times k\). If \(d\) is a factor of \(24\), then \(24 = d\times m\) for a whole number \(m\), so the count is \(24\times k = d\times(mk)\), and that shows \(d\) divides the count. In words, whatever divides \(24\) divides every multiple of \(24\), and the inheritance only flows downward to \(24\)'s own factors. Run the six sizes through this test. Since \(2,\ 3,\ 4,\) and \(8\) are all on the factor list of \(24\), the count must be a multiple of each of them. But \(9\) is not a factor of \(24\), because \(24 = 9\times 2 + 6\) leaves a remainder of \(6\), and \(16\) is not a factor either, since \(24 = 16 + 8\) leaves a remainder of \(8\). So neither \(9\) nor \(16\) is guaranteed, and the cleanest counterexample for both is \(24\) itself, which is a multiple of \(24\) yet is divisible by neither \(9\) nor \(16\). Collecting the guaranteed sizes gives $$\boxed{2,\ 3,\ 4,\ 8}.$$ Check the trickiest survivor, \(8\), on a real multiple, \(24\times 5 = 120 = 8\times 15\), divisible just as promised, while \(120\) is not a multiple of \(16\) since \(120 = 16\times 7 + 8\). The general idea is that being a multiple of \(n\) only forces divisibility by the factors of \(n\), so divisibility chains downward through \(n\)'s factor list and never reaches a number that is not a factor of \(n\).

review.x: A museum is engraving a new lock for its gem cabinet, and the curator wants the secret code to be a number with two pleasing properties at once. It must be a perfect square, so the gems can be set in a neat square array, and it must be a multiple of 45, since the cabinet has 45 little compartments and each must hold the same whole number of gems. The curator also insists the code stay below 2000 to fit the dial. Find every number that works, and be ready to argue that none slipped past you. How many such numbers are there? Enter that count.
hints: A multiple of 45 must carry the factors hiding inside 45. Break 45 into primes and ask what the number is forced to contain.
Write the square as \(s \times s\). If the product \(s\times s\) must contain a single factor of 5 and a factor of 9, what is the side \(s\) forced to contain?
Once you see that \(s\) must be a multiple of 15, only check \(15^2\), \(30^2\), \(45^2\), and stop the moment you pass 2000.
review.sol: First listen to what "multiple of 45" really demands. Since \(45 = 9 \times 5 = 3^2 \times 5\), every multiple of 45 must carry at least two factors of 3 and one factor of 5. Now suppose the code is a perfect square, so I can write it as \(s \times s\) for some whole number \(s\). The clever part is to track each prime through that product. The square needs a factor of 5, but 5 is a prime, so the two copies of \(s\) cannot share it loosely; for \(s\times s\) to contain a 5 at all, one of the copies of \(s\) must contain a 5, and then the other copy contains one too, so the square actually carries \(5\times 5\). The same pressure works for 3. The square needs a factor of 3, and since one copy of \(s\) must supply it, both copies do, so the square carries \(3 \times 3\) just from a single 3 living in \(s\). Putting those together, \(s\) must contain a 3 and a 5, which means \(s\) is a multiple of \(3 \times 5 = 15\), and therefore the square is a multiple of \(15^2 = 225\). That is the quiet twist, the two separate demands for a 9 and a 5 combine and force the code to be a multiple of 225, not merely of 45. Now I only have to walk through the multiples of 15 and square them, watching the 2000 ceiling. $$15^2 = 225,\qquad 30^2 = 900,\qquad 45^2 = 2025$$ The first two sit safely below 2000, but \(45^2 = 2025\) overshoots, and every later square is larger still, so the search is complete. Nothing was left out, because any valid code had to be \(15^2\) times a perfect square, and \(225 \times 1\) and \(225 \times 4\) are the only such values under 2000. The codes are \(\boxed{225 \text{ and } 900}\). Check that each truly fits, since \(225 = 15^2\) is a square and \(225 = 45 \times 5\) is a multiple of 45, while \(900 = 30^2\) is a square and \(900 = 45 \times 20\) is a multiple of 45. The general idea is that a perfect square must contain every prime to an even power, so demanding a square that is a multiple of a number forces the square to be a multiple of that number's "squared-up" version, here 45 rounding up to 225.

review.x: Here is something lovely hiding inside a number's own factors. Take \(6\) and list every factor of it that is smaller than \(6\) itself, which gives \(1\), \(2\), and \(3\). Add them and you land right back where you started, since \(1+2+3=6\). A number that rebuilds itself this way out of its own smaller factors is called a perfect number. The next one is \(28\), whose smaller factors are \(1\), \(2\), \(4\), \(7\), and \(14\), and sure enough \(1+2+4+7+14=28\). They are rare and they grow far apart fast, the one after \(28\) is already \(496\). Every perfect number anyone has ever found is even, and here is the part that still keeps mathematicians up at night. Nobody knows whether an odd perfect number exists. People have searched past numbers with hundreds of digits and found none, yet no one has managed to prove one can never turn up. So the next time you factor a number, remember you might be poking at one of the oldest unanswered questions in all of mathematics.

blocks.x: In the last lesson we picked up two quiet ideas that let us reason about multiples without grinding through arithmetic. Closure told us that adding or taking away a multiple of \(n\) leaves us standing on another multiple of \(n\), and chaining told us that a multiple of a multiple carries along every factor hiding underneath it. Both of those let us know something about a number without ever doing the heavy work of dividing it out. So here is a fair question. Suppose someone hands you a big number like 6,351 and asks whether 3 goes into it evenly, or whether 4 does. Must you really set up the long division, carry, and scratch your way down to the remainder every single time, or is there a way to glance at the number and simply know? There is a faster way, and the secret lives in place value. A number is nothing more than its digits sitting in places worth \(1\), \(10\), \(100\), \(1000\), and on upward, and each of those place values carries its own divisibility along with it. That is the opening we are going to pry at. We will cut a number apart at a clever spot, hand one of the chunks over to closure so it quietly vanishes, and read the answer off the small piece that is left behind. Let us go find that clever spot.

A cloakroom hands out claim tags one after another, numbered in the order coats arrive, and the attendant slips a small prize behind every tag whose number is a multiple of 5. Five coats come back with these tags, 2,470, 3,805, 6,118, 9,640, and 7,333. Without dividing any of them out the long way, decide which tags win a prize and note which single digit of a tag number settles the whole question. As a finishing touch, think about which winning tags are also multiples of 10. How many of the five tags win a prize? Enter that count.
hints: You do not have to divide the whole tag by 5. Write one tag as a stack of tens plus its leftover ones digit, like 2,470 = 247 tens and 0 ones, and watch which part you can stop worrying about.

A whole number of tens is already a multiple of 5, since every ten is 5 twice. By closure from the last lesson, throwing away a multiple of 5 leaves the question resting on the single ones digit. Which ones digits make the leftover a multiple of 5?
Sort the tags by their last digit alone. A tag wins for 5 when it ends in 0 or 5, and it also wins for 10 only when it ends in 0.
blocks.sol: Take any tag and cut it right after the tens, so the ones digit stands alone. Every tag then reads as a whole number of tens plus that single leftover, $$N = 10k + u,$$ where \(k\) is everything above the ones place and \(u\) is the ones digit itself. The first piece, \(10k\), is a whole number of tens, and since one ten is \(5\times 2\), that piece is always a multiple of \(5\) no matter how large \(k\) is. By closure, adding or removing a multiple of \(5\) cannot change whether the total is a multiple of \(5\), so the prize question slides entirely onto the leftover \(u\). A tag wins for \(5\) exactly when its ones digit is \(0\) or \(5\). Reading just the last digits, \(2{,}470\) ends in \(0\), \(3{,}805\) ends in \(5\), \(6{,}118\) ends in \(8\), \(9{,}640\) ends in \(0\), and \(7{,}333\) ends in \(3\). The winners are \(\boxed{2{,}470,\ 3{,}805,\ 9{,}640}\). The same split tells the \(10\) story for free, because \(10k\) is already a clean stack of tens, so a tag is a multiple of \(10\) only when the leftover \(u\) is \(0\), which singles out \(2{,}470\) and \(9{,}640\). Check \(3{,}805 = 5\times 761\), a genuine multiple of \(5\) but not of \(10\), just as its final \(5\) promised. The general idea is that the ones digit carries the whole verdict for \(5\) and \(10\), since everything above it is a pile of tens that closure lets us ignore.

blocks.x: A marching band director needs the whole band to line up in perfect pairs, two players to a row, so a formation only works when the head count is even. Four different sections report their numbers, the brass with 84,317 players, the percussion with 2,000,006, the woodwinds with 55,550, and the color guard with 9,999. (Yes, these are wildly oversized bands, but play along.) Decide which of these counts can line up in perfect pairs with nobody left standing alone. Try not to do any long division on those huge numbers, and as you work, notice how much of each number you can ignore. How many of the four counts can pair up perfectly? Enter that count.
hints: Pairing up two to a row works exactly when the count is even, so the real question is which of these four counts is even. Look hard at the very end of each number before you reach for anything bigger.

Try writing a number as its tens part plus its last digit, like \(55{,}550 = 55{,}55 \times 10 + 0\) or \(84{,}317 = 8{,}431 \times 10 + 7\). Any whole number of tens is already even, so the only thing still in doubt is that final leftover digit.
For \(2{,}000{,}006\), all those zeros sit in the tens part and are already even on their own. Only the final \(6\) is left to decide the whole question, and \(6\) is even.
blocks.sol: Lining up two to a row with nobody alone is exactly asking whether each count is even, so I want to test evenness without wrestling those giant numbers. The trick is to break each count at the spot where everything to the left becomes a clean number of tens. Write any whole number as its tens part plus its single units digit, \(N = 10k + u\), where \(u\) is that last digit. The piece \(10k\) is ten times a whole number, and ten is even, so \(10k\) is even no matter how enormous \(k\) is. An even pile already splits perfectly into pairs by itself. That means the entire fate of \(N\) rests on the leftover \(u\), since adding an even chunk cannot change whether the total is even or odd. So \(N\) is even exactly when its units digit \(u\) is even. $$N = 10k + u, \qquad 10k \text{ is always even, so even-ness rides on } u$$ Now sweep the four bands, glancing only at the last digit. Brass \(84{,}317\) ends in \(7\), odd, so one player is always left without a partner. Percussion \(2{,}000{,}006\) ends in \(6\), and here is the surprise, the whole \(2{,}000{,}000\) in front is a flat number of tens and is already even all on its own, so every one of those zeros is silent and only the final \(6\) speaks, which is even, so it pairs. Woodwinds \(55{,}550\) ends in \(0\), and \(0\) is even, so it pairs cleanly too. Color guard \(9{,}999\) ends in \(9\), odd, so it cannot. The counts that line up in perfect pairs are $$\boxed{2{,}000{,}006 \text{ and } 55{,}550}.$$ Check, \(55{,}550 = 27{,}775 \times 2\) and \(2{,}000{,}006 = 1{,}000{,}003 \times 2\), both genuine doubles, while \(84{,}317\) and \(9{,}999\) each leave a remainder of \(1\) when you try. The general idea is that the tens part of any number is already a multiple of \(2\), so evenness is decided by the units digit alone, and all the other digits, however many millions they stretch across, simply do not matter.

blocks.x: Last-digit tests for 10, 5, and 2. Split any whole number into its last digit and everything above it. If the units digit is \(u\), then the part to its left counts a whole number of tens, so $$N = 10k + u,$$ where \(k\) is the number you read off the digits before the units. The \(10k\) part is what makes these tests work, since \(10\) already carries a factor of \(2\) and a factor of \(5\), which makes \(10k\) a multiple of \(2\), of \(5\), and of \(10\) all at once. By closure, adding a multiple like that cannot change whether \(N\) is divisible by \(2\), \(5\), or \(10\), so the whole decision falls on the units digit \(u\). That hands you three clean tests. A number is divisible by 10 exactly when its units digit is \(0\), since that is the only single digit which is itself a multiple of \(10\). A number is divisible by 5 exactly when its units digit is \(0\) or \(5\), the only digits that are multiples of \(5\). And a number is divisible by 2 exactly when its units digit is even, that is \(0, 2, 4, 6,\) or \(8\). The shared idea is that everything above the units is a tidy bundle of tens, and tens already hold the factors \(2\) and \(5\), so only the last digit is ever in doubt.

The last digit settled divisibility by 2, by 5, and by 10 because ten itself is a multiple of all three. Every digit above the units sits on a multiple of ten, and closure lets us sweep that whole part away, leaving only the units digit to check. Now try the same move on 4. Take a number like 70 and pull it apart into \( 7 \times 10 + 0 \), then ask whether closure can throw the tens part away. It cannot, because \( 4 \) does not divide \( 10 \), so the \( 7 \times 10 \) chunk is not a multiple of 4 and stays put. The units digit on its own cannot decide this one. But nothing says we have to cut a number right before the units. We could cut it earlier and keep a slightly bigger piece to test, as long as everything above the cut lands on a multiple of 4 so closure can sweep it away like before. So where should the cut go? See if you can find the spot where the upper part finally becomes a clean multiple of 4.

A card dealer is splitting a stack of cards into complete hands of exactly 4 cards each, which only works when the total deals out evenly, with no card left over. Tonight four different stacks come to the table, of sizes 5,328, 7,914, 13,500, and 60,742. Rather than dividing these big numbers, peel each one apart at the hundreds, writing it as a whole number of hundreds plus the number sitting in its last two digits, like \(5{,}328 = 53\times100 + 28\). Since \(100\) itself splits into complete hands of \(4\), think about what that does to the hundreds part, and decide which stacks deal out into full hands of \(4\). How many of the four stacks work? Enter that count.
hints: Try splitting each deck at the hundreds, like \(7{,}914 = 79\times100 + 14\). One hundred is \(25\) full hands of \(4\), so every full hundred already deals out evenly. That means the hundreds part never causes trouble, no matter how big it is.

If the hundreds part always deals out evenly, then whether the whole stack works comes down to that small leftover number in the last two digits. So only check whether \(28\), \(14\), \(00\), and \(42\) each split into hands of \(4\).
blocks.sol: The clever move is to cut each deck at the hundreds, since a hundred behaves beautifully here. One hundred is a whole number of hands, because $$100 = 4\times25,$$ so every full hundred cards deals out into exactly \(25\) complete hands of \(4\) with nothing left over. Now peel any stack apart at its last two digits, writing it as a pile of full hundreds plus the leftover. Take the first deck, \(5{,}328 = 53\times100 + 28\). The \(53\times100\) part is \(53\) hundreds, and each hundred is already a multiple of \(4\), so that whole chunk deals out evenly on its own. By closure, adding or removing such a clean chunk cannot change whether the total works, so the entire stack deals out evenly exactly when the leftover \(28\) does. That is the discovery, everything above the last two digits is full hundreds and can be ignored, so only the last two digits decide it. Run the four decks through this lens. $$5{,}328 = 53\times100 + 28,\qquad 7{,}914 = 79\times100 + 14$$ $$13{,}500 = 135\times100 + 00,\qquad 60{,}742 = 607\times100 + 42$$ The hundreds chunk is harmless every time, so check only the leftover. For \(5{,}328\) the leftover \(28 = 4\times7\) deals out, so it works. For \(7{,}914\) the leftover \(14\) is not a multiple of \(4\), since \(4\times3 = 12\) and \(4\times4 = 16\) skip right over it, so it fails. For \(13{,}500\) the leftover is \(00\), an empty leftover that needs no extra hand at all, so it works. For \(60{,}742\) the leftover \(42\) lands between \(40\) and \(44\), so it fails. The decks that deal out into full hands of \(4\) are $$\boxed{5{,}328 \text{ and } 13{,}500}.$$ Check the close one, \(5{,}328 = 4\times1332\), a tidy \(1332\) hands. The general idea is that since \(100\) is a multiple of \(4\), divisibility by \(4\) is decided entirely by the last two digits, because every higher place is a full bundle of hundreds that closure lets you throw away.

blocks.x: Divisibility by 4. A whole number is divisible by \(4\) exactly when the number formed by its last two digits is divisible by \(4\). The reason is place value. Split any number into its last two digits plus everything from the hundreds place up, like \(8{,}516 = 8{,}500 + 16\). That front chunk, here \(8{,}500\), is always a whole number of hundreds, and since \(100 = 4 \times 25\) is a multiple of \(4\), a whole number of hundreds is a multiple of \(4\) too. So the front chunk is already a multiple of \(4\), and by closure it falls away without changing divisibility, which leaves only the last two digits to check.

An arcade dispenses prize tokens only in sealed strips of 8, never a loose token. Three machines flash their lifetime totals on a screen, 47,216 then 9,360 then 128,500, and a player wants to know which of these totals could have come out as whole strips with none left over. Instead of dividing each giant number by 8, cover everything except the last three digits of each total and test only that small tail. Then say why it takes three digits to settle 8, when the last lesson settled 4 with only two. How many of the three totals are divisible by 8? Enter that count.
hints: A whole number of strips means the total is a multiple of \(8\). Look at \(1000\). Can you write \(1000\) itself as a whole number times \(8\)? If every full thousand is already a stack of strips, then the thousands part can be thrown away and only what is left below a thousand can decide things.
