The general idea is that switching off each number's higher multiples in order leaves exactly the primes standing, and because every composite up to a limit must carry a prime factor no larger than the square root of that limit, you only need to sweep with the primes up to that square root.

blocks.x:
Here is the key idea that makes the corridor sweep work. Every composite number has a smallest prime factor, and that smallest prime factor is never larger than the square root of the number itself. The reason is the same factor-pair idea from 3.1. If a number \(N\) is composite, you can write it as \(N = a \times b\) with both \(a\) and \(b\) bigger than \(1\), and the two partners of a pair sit on opposite sides of \(\sqrt{N}\), so the smaller one is at most \(\sqrt{N}\). Break that smaller partner down into its own prime factors and the very smallest prime hiding inside \(N\) is at most \(\sqrt{N}\) too. So when the caretakers sweep door by door, every composite door is switched off by a prime caretaker no larger than the square root of that door. That means you never need a caretaker larger than \(\sqrt{N}\) to clear every composite up to \(N\).

$$\text{If } N \text{ is composite, its smallest prime factor} \le \sqrt{N}.$$

This is why the sweepers can stop early. To leave only the primes among all the doors up to \(N\), you only have to send out the prime caretakers up to \(\sqrt{N}\). They erase every composite, and the doors still standing are exactly the primes.

Look at the two primes \(23\) and \(29\). They sit only six apart, like two fence posts with a stretch of open field between them. Now walk through every whole number strictly between them, one step at a time. There are five of them in your path, namely \(24, 25, 26, 27, 28\). For each one, try to catch it being composite by naming a factor other than \(1\) and itself. Is \(24\) even? Does \(25\) split as \(5 \times 5\)? What about \(26\), \(27\), and \(28\)? Once you have checked all five, answer this single question. How many prime numbers lie strictly between \(23\) and \(29\)?
hints: A number is prime only when its sole factors are \(1\) and itself. So go down the list \(24, 25, 26, 27, 28\) and try to break each one into a product of smaller whole numbers.
Three of these five are even, so \(2\) divides them right away. For the two odd ones, test \(25\) against \(5\) and test \(27\) against \(3\).
If every single number between \(23\) and \(29\) turns out to be composite, then none of them is prime. Count how many primes survived.
blocks.sol:
A prime is a whole number greater than \(1\) whose only factors are \(1\) and itself. To find the primes strictly between \(23\) and \(29\), you only need to inspect the five numbers in that gap and see whether any of them refuses to be broken apart. Here is each one caught in the act of being composite, with a factor named for each.

$$24 = 2 \times 12, \qquad 25 = 5 \times 5, \qquad 26 = 2 \times 13, \qquad 27 = 3 \times 9, \qquad 28 = 2 \times 14$$

Every one of them factors into smaller whole numbers, so every one of them is composite. The three even numbers \(24\), \(26\), and \(28\) are handed to you by the divisibility test for \(2\) from section 3.2. The odd ones give themselves up just as easily, since \(25\) ends in \(5\) and \(27 = 3 \times 3 \times 3\). Not a single prime hides among \(24, 25, 26, 27, 28\). So the count of primes strictly between \(23\) and \(29\) is \(\boxed{0}\).

Quick check. Run back through the list and confirm each number has a factor pair besides \(1\) times itself. \(24\) is even, \(25 = 5 \times 5\), \(26\) is even, \(27 = 3 \times 3 \times 3\), \(28\) is even. Five composites in a row, zero primes, which matches our answer.

Notice what just happened. The primes \(23\) and \(29\) left a run of five composite numbers between them with no prime in sight. Primes do not march along at a steady, even spacing. They cluster in some places and leave wide gaps in others. And here is the surprising part to carry forward. These gaps are not capped at five. With the right construction you can build a stretch of one hundred composite numbers in a row, or a thousand, or a million, with no prime anywhere inside. The general idea is that primes thin out unpredictably, leaving prime-free gaps that can be stretched as long as you like.

blocks.x: Marta has been staring at the number 223 and wants to settle one question once and for all. Is it prime, or does it secretly hide a factor pair? She remembers from section 3.1 that any factor of a number comes with a partner, and the smaller member of every pair sits at or below the square root. Since \(15 \times 15 = 225\), the square root of 223 is just under 15, so the smaller partner of any factor pair would have to be somewhere from 2 up to 14. That means she only has to hunt for a divisor in that small range, and she decides to be clever and test only the primes there, in increasing order, stopping the moment her stopping line tells her she is done. She checks 2 (223 is odd), then 3 (its digit sum \(2+2+3=7\) is not a multiple of 3), then 5 (it ends in 3, not 0 or 5), then she divides by 7, then by 11, then by 13, each time finding a leftover. The next prime after 13 is 17, and that one is already past her square-root line, so she puts down her pencil. What is the largest number Marta ever has to divide by during this whole hunt?
hints: Marta only needs to look for a factor whose partner is at least as big, so the smaller one sits at or below the square root of 223. Since \(15 \times 15 = 225\), that square root is just under 15. How far up does she actually have to test?
She is smart and tests only primes, because any composite trial divisor (like 9 or 15) is built from smaller primes she already checked. List the primes from 2 up to 14 and find the biggest one.
The primes she tests are 2, 3, 5, 7, 11, and 13. The next prime, 17, is past her stopping line, so it never gets used. Which of the primes she actually divides by is the largest?
blocks.sol:
Marta's plan rests on the factor-pair idea from 3.1. If 223 had a factor, that factor would come with a partner so that the two multiply to 223, and the smaller of the pair can be no larger than the square root of 223. Since \(14 \times 14 = 196\) and \(15 \times 15 = 225\), the square root of 223 lands between 14 and 15. So any hidden small factor must live somewhere from 2 up to 14, and that is her stopping line. The next clever move is to test only primes in that range. Why only primes? Because every composite number in 2 through 14, like 4, 6, 8, 9, 10, 12, or 14, is built out of smaller primes. If 223 were divisible by 9, it would already be divisible by 3, which she checked first. So a composite trial divisor can never catch a factor that the smaller primes missed. The primes from 2 up to 14 are 2, 3, 5, 7, 11, and 13. Marta marches through them using the tools from 3.2 and a little division. $$223 	ext{ is odd, so } 2 
mid 223$$ $$2+2+3 = 7, 	ext{ not a multiple of } 3, 	ext{ so } 3 
mid 223$$ $$223 	ext{ ends in } 3, 	ext{ so } 5 
mid 223$$ $$223 = 7 	imes 31 + 6, 	ext{ so } 7 
mid 223$$ $$223 	ext{ alternating sum } 3 - 2 + 2 = 3, 	ext{ not a multiple of } 11, 	ext{ so } 11 
mid 223$$ $$223 = 13 	imes 17 + 2, 	ext{ so } 13 
mid 223$$ The next prime is 17, but \(17 > 15\), so it is already past the square-root line. There is nothing left below the line to test. Every prime up to the stopping line has failed to divide 223, so 223 has no factor pair other than \(1 \times 223\), which means 223 is prime. Now the actual question. The largest number she ever divides by is the largest prime she tests, and that is 13. The boxed answer is \(\boxed{13}\). Quick check. The primes she tested are 2, 3, 5, 7, 11, 13, and the biggest of those is indeed 13. The next prime, 17, would have a partner smaller than itself if it divided 223, and that smaller partner would already have been caught, so 17 never needs a turn. Confirmed. The general idea is that to decide whether a number is prime, you only have to test prime divisors up to its square root, because any factor larger than that is partnered with a smaller one you have already checked.

blocks.x: Here is the test you just discovered. To check whether a number \(n\) is prime, try dividing it by the primes \(2, 3, 5, 7, 11, 13\) and so on in order, and the moment a prime divisor climbs past \(\sqrt{n}\) you may stop. If none of those primes divided \(n\) evenly, then \(n\) is prime. Two ideas from earlier chapters work together to make this safe. From 3.1, every factor pairs with a partner, and the two partners sit on opposite sides of \(\sqrt{n}\), so if \(n\) has any factor at all then one of the pair must land at or below \(\sqrt{n}\). And you only ever test primes because, by the chaining idea from 3.2, any composite trial divisor is itself built out of smaller primes you have already tried, so it can never catch \(n\) when those smaller primes did not. To move fast, clear \(2, 3, 5\), and \(11\) at a glance with the 3.2 divisibility tests before you bother with long division.

All through the last section we held a number still and tested it, slicing off a chunk and asking whether some divisor went in cleanly. Now we turn that whole habit inside out. Instead of judging a finished number, we are going to take it apart and watch what falls out. Remember the unbreakable numbers, the ones whose only factors are \(1\) and themselves, with no rectangle but a single long line. Those are the primes, and the rest, the composites, are exactly the numbers that do split, since each one offers a rectangle with two sides bigger than \(1\). Here is the move that carries this lesson. Whenever a number is composite, write it as a product of two smaller factors, then look at each of those factors and split again any that is still composite, and keep going. Each cut hands you smaller numbers, so the splitting cannot run on forever, and it only stops when every piece left is prime and nothing can be broken further. That branching picture, one number sending down two factors, each of those sending down two more, is what people call a factor tree, and it leaves a little crop of primes hanging at the bottom. Primes start to look less like oddities and more like the atoms of arithmetic, the building blocks that every composite is assembled from. But a sharp worry should be tugging at you. If two people start prying the same number apart and make different first cuts, do they always reach the same atoms at the bottom, or could the pile of primes you end up with depend on how you chose to chop? The next problem settles it.

Two friends sit down to break the number \(90\) all the way down into prime atoms, and they start with totally different first cuts.

Tara looks at \(90\) and sees \(90 = 9 \times 10\). She keeps splitting any piece that is not yet prime. Since \(9 = 3 \times 3\) and \(10 = 2 \times 5\), her tree bottoms out at the atoms \(2\), \(3\), \(3\), and \(5\).

Niko looks at the same \(90\) and instead writes \(90 = 6 \times 15\). He splits too, with \(6 = 2 \times 3\) and \(15 = 3 \times 5\), and his tree bottoms out at the atoms \(2\), \(3\), \(3\), and \(5\).

They picked different doorways into the same number, yet they landed on the exact same pile of primes. Counting every atom that lands at the bottom, and counting a repeated prime each time it shows up, how many prime atoms does \(90\) have?
hints: A prime atom is a number you cannot split any further with whole-number factors bigger than 1, like \(2\), \(3\), or \(5\). Keep splitting until every piece is one of those.
Both friends end with the same little collection of primes. Line up Tara's bottom row and Niko's bottom row and check that they match.
Count the primes at the bottom one by one, and if the same prime appears twice, count it twice.
blocks.sol:
The big surprise here is that the first cut does not matter. Tara opened \(90\) as \(9 \times 10\) and Niko opened it as \(6 \times 15\), and you might expect two different answers. Let us walk both trees down to the bottom in words and see what really happens.

Tara's route splits \(90 = 9 \times 10\). The \(9\) is not prime, so it breaks into \(3 \times 3\). The \(10\) is not prime, so it breaks into \(2 \times 5\). Everything left, namely \(2\), \(3\), \(3\), \(5\), is prime, so she stops. Her atoms are \(2\), \(3\), \(3\), \(5\).

Niko's route splits \(90 = 6 \times 15\). The \(6\) breaks into \(2 \times 3\), and the \(15\) breaks into \(3 \times 5\). Again every leftover piece is prime, so he stops. His atoms are \(2\), \(3\), \(3\), \(5\).

The two bottom rows are identical. Different doorways, the very same bag of atoms. Now we just count them, remembering to count the repeated \(3\) both times it appears.

$$90 = 2 \times 3 \times 3 \times 5 = 2 \times 3^2 \times 5$$

Counting with repeats gives one \(2\), two \(3\)s, and one \(5\), so the total number of prime atoms is

$$1 + 2 + 1 = \boxed{4}$$

Quick check: multiply the atoms back together. \(2 \times 3 \times 3 \times 5 = 6 \times 15 = 90\). It rebuilds the original number, so nothing was lost or added.

The general idea is that no matter which factor pair you start with, a number always breaks down into the one same multiset of primes, and that shared bag of atoms is its prime factorization.

blocks.x: Here is the big idea you have been circling. Every whole number above 1 can be written as a product of primes, and apart from the order you write the factors, there is exactly one way to do it. Those primes are the number's unique fingerprint, and we call this product its prime factorization. We pack repeated primes into exponent shorthand, so for example \(90 = 2 \times 3^2 \times 5\), where \(3^2\) just means \(3 \times 3\). Why is the fingerprint always the same no matter how you start? Because if you always pull out the smallest prime that divides what is left, every single step is forced and you have no choices to make, so there is no room for two different bags of prime atoms to multiply to the same number. That is the Fundamental Theorem of Arithmetic, and it is what makes primes the true building blocks of every whole number.

The left tree splits 72 into 8 and 9, then 8 into 2 and 4 and 4 into 2 and 2, while 9 splits into 3 and 3. The right tree splits 72 into 6 and 12, then 6 into 2 and 3, and 12 into 3 and 4 and 4 into 2 and 2. The prime leaves of both trees are the same, three 2s and two 3s, so both trees reach the fingerprint 2 cubed times 3 squared.">
