blocks.cap: Look at how the two trees begin in completely different ways. On the left we first cut 72 into 8 and 9, while on the right we first cut it into 6 and 12, and along the way that 12 even splits again through a 4. Yet no matter which first cut you choose, both trees funnel all the way down to the very same atoms, namely two, two, two, three, three. That shared fingerprint is \(2^3 \times 3^2\), and it shows the uniqueness from the key idea above, since every honest factor tree for 72 has to land on exactly this collection of primes.

blocks.x:
A vault in the school science museum has a locked display that guards a single whole number. For security the display refuses to show the number on its face. Instead it prints only the number's fingerprint, the exact list of prime atoms that multiply together to build it. Today the fingerprint glows like this,

$$2 \times 3 \times 3 \times 7$$

A plaque underneath promises that every number has exactly one such fingerprint, so this list points to one and only one hidden number. Multiply the atoms back together and rebuild the number the display is hiding. What is that number?
hints: The fingerprint is just a multiplication waiting to happen. You do not need to divide anything, you only need to multiply the listed atoms together.
Group the easy pieces first. The two 3s make \(3 \times 3 = 9\), which leaves you with \(2 \times 9 \times 7\).
Multiply in any order you like, since the order never changes a product. Try \(2 \times 9 = 18\) first, then multiply that by 7.
blocks.sol:
The fingerprint is not a riddle, it is a recipe. It tells you the exact prime atoms that snap together to make the hidden number, so rebuilding it just means doing the multiplication.

You can pair the factors in whatever order is comfortable. The two 3s combine into \(3 \\times 3 = 9\), and then you finish the job:

$$2 \times 3 \times 3 \times 7 = 2 \times 9 \times 7 = 18 \times 7 = 126$$

So the display is hiding the number \(\\boxed{126}\).

Here is the part worth slowing down for. You can read facts about 126 straight off its fingerprint, without ever dividing. There is a lone 2 sitting in the list, and from section 3.2 a number is even exactly when it carries a factor of 2, so 126 must be even. There are also two 3s, which together form a \(3^2 = 9\), and a number is divisible by 9 exactly when it carries that pair of 3s, so 126 must be divisible by 9. The fingerprint announced both facts before you computed a thing.

Quick check. Is 126 really even? Yes, it ends in 6. Is it really divisible by 9? Add its digits, \(1 + 2 + 6 = 9\), and 9 is divisible by 9, so it passes the divisibility test from 3.2.

The general idea is that a number's prime fingerprint is more than a way to rebuild it, it is a label that lets you read off divisibility facts by simply spotting which prime atoms are present.

blocks.x: A craftsperson has 360 small glass beads and wants to arrange every single one of them into a perfect square, the same number of rows as columns, with none left over. She tries for a while and gets frustrated, because 360 is just shy of fitting. You can check her trouble quickly using the fingerprint from section 3.1, the prime factorization \(360 = 2^3 \times 3^2 \times 5\). Rather than give up, she decides to order a few more beads of the exact same kind, just enough so that the new total is a perfect square. She wants to buy as few extra beads as possible, and the new total has to be a whole-number multiple of her original 360 (so the new pile is \(360\) times some whole number). What is the smallest whole number she can multiply 360 by so that the result is a perfect square?
hints: Look at the fingerprint \(360 = 2^3 \times 3^2 \times 5\). When you build a square of side \(s\), each side gets exactly half of every prime in the total. So think about which exponents can be split into two equal whole pieces and which cannot.

An exponent like the 2 on the \(3\) splits evenly into \(1\) and \(1\). But the 3 on the \(2\) and the hidden 1 on the \(5\) are odd, so they cannot be cut cleanly in half. Each odd exponent is the real problem.
To turn an odd exponent into an even one, you only need one more copy of that prime. Find every prime with an odd exponent, and multiply by exactly one of each. Then ask what that product is.
blocks.sol:
Start from the fingerprint, since the primes tell the whole story. We have
$$ 360 = 2^3 \times 3^2 \times 5. $$
Now picture building a square out of the whole pile. If the total is \(s \times s\), then every prime in the total has to be shared equally between the two equal sides, which means each prime must split into two identical halves. A prime can be cut cleanly in half exactly when its exponent is even, because then half the copies go to one side and the other half go to the other. So let us check each exponent.

The \(3\) appears with exponent \(2\), and \(2\) is even, so the \(3\) is already happy, one copy to each side. The \(2\) appears with exponent \(3\), which is odd, so it cannot be split evenly. The \(5\) appears with exponent \(1\), also odd, so it cannot be split evenly either. Those two odd exponents are exactly why 360 refuses to be a square.

To fix an odd exponent, nudge it up to the next even number by tossing in one more copy of that prime. The \(2^3\) needs one more \(2\) to become \(2^4\), and the \(5^1\) needs one more \(5\) to become \(5^2\). The \(3^2\) is already even, so we leave it alone. The smallest extra factor is therefore
$$ 2 \times 5 = 10. $$
Multiplying gives
$$ 360 \times 10 = 3600 = 2^4 \times 3^2 \times 5^2, $$
and every exponent is now even. The side of the square is one half of each, namely \(2^2 \times 3^1 \times 5^1 = 60\), so
$$ 3600 = 60 \times 60 = 60^2. $$
The smallest whole number to multiply by is \(\boxed{10}\).

Quick check. \(60 \times 60 = 3600\), and \(3600 \div 360 = 10\), so the new pile really is \(360\) times \(10\) and really is a perfect square. No smaller multiplier works, because any multiplier that left out the extra \(2\) or the extra \(5\) would leave one of those exponents odd.

The general idea is that a number is a perfect square exactly when every exponent in its prime fingerprint is even, so to reach the nearest square you supply one more copy of each prime that currently appears an odd number of times.

blocks.x: A toy company switches from flat tiles to solid building blocks. They want to stack their leftover unit cubes into one perfect cube, a solid block that is \(s\) units wide, \(s\) units deep, and \(s\) units tall. The pile they have on hand holds exactly \(144\) unit cubes, and you already know its prime fingerprint is \(2^4 \times 3^2\). A perfect cube of cubes is built from three identical layers stacked on top of each other, and each of those layers is itself a flat \(s \times s\) square, so the whole thing is three equal copies of the same square pile multiplied together. With \(144\) cubes you cannot finish a perfect cube, so the company will buy a few more unit cubes and multiply the count up to the next perfect cube. They do not want to overspend, so they want the smallest jump possible. What is the smallest positive whole number you can multiply \(144\) by so that the result is a perfect cube?
hints: A perfect square wanted every prime to show up an even number of times so it could split into two equal groups. A perfect cube splits into three equal groups instead. How many times must each prime appear so it can be shared evenly among three layers?

Look at the fingerprint \(2^4 \times 3^2\). For a cube, each exponent has to be a multiple of \(3\). The exponent \(4\) is not a multiple of \(3\), and neither is \(2\). Climb each one up to the next multiple of \(3\).

The next multiple of \(3\) above \(4\) is \(6\), so you are short two \(2\)s. The next multiple of \(3\) above \(2\) is \(3\), so you are short one \(3\). Multiply those missing primes together to find your number.
blocks.sol:
Start by remembering what a perfect square needed. To arrange \(n\) cubes into a flat \(s \times s\) square, you split the primes into two equal groups, one for each side of the square, so every prime had to appear an even number of times. A perfect cube is the same idea taken one step further. A solid \(s \times s \times s\) block is three equal copies of the side length multiplied together, so the primes have to split into three equal groups. That means each prime must appear a number of times that is a multiple of \(3\), one third going into each dimension.

Now look at the fingerprint you were handed.
$$144 = 2^4 \times 3^2$$
Check each exponent against the nearest multiple of \(3\) from above. The prime \(2\) appears \(4\) times, but \(4\) is not a multiple of \(3\). The multiples of \(3\) go \(3, 6, 9, \dots\), and the first one at or above \(4\) is \(6\), so the exponent on \(2\) needs to climb from \(4\) to \(6\). That is two more \(2\)s. The prime \(3\) appears \(2\) times, and the first multiple of \(3\) at or above \(2\) is \(3\), so the exponent on \(3\) needs to climb from \(2\) to \(3\). That is one more \(3\).

Collect exactly the missing primes and multiply them together.
$$2^2 \times 3 = 4 \times 3 = 12$$
So multiplying by \(12\) is what lifts every exponent up to its next multiple of \(3\).

Quick check. Multiply it out.
$$144 \times 12 = 2^4 \times 3^2 \times 2^2 \times 3 = 2^6 \times 3^3$$
Both exponents are now multiples of \(3\), and splitting them three ways gives \(2^2 \times 3 = 12\) per side, so the block is \(12 \times 12 \times 12 = 1728\). It really is a perfect cube, and no smaller multiplier could have fixed both shortfalls at once.

The smallest positive number is \(\boxed{12}\).

The general idea is that a number is a perfect cube exactly when every exponent in its prime fingerprint is a multiple of \(3\), and the cheapest way to reach the next cube is to push each exponent up to its next multiple of \(3\).

blocks.x: Here is the fingerprint you just uncovered. Take any number, write its prime factorization, and look only at the exponents. The number is a perfect square exactly when every exponent is even, since taking the square root just halves each one. It is a perfect cube exactly when every exponent is a multiple of 3, since the cube root takes a third of each. Both are really one idea. A number is a perfect \(k\)-th power exactly when every exponent in its prime factorization is divisible by \(k\), because the \(k\)-th root simply divides each exponent by \(k\), and that only lands on a whole number when the exponent was a multiple of \(k\) to begin with.

A friend hands you a slip of paper that does not show a number at all. Instead it shows the number's prime fingerprint, the exact recipe of primes that multiply together to build it:

$$2^4 \times 3 \times 5^6.$$

Your friend dares you to answer one question without ever multiplying the whole thing out and without a calculator. The question is this. When that hidden number is finally written out in full as ordinary digits, how many zeros does it end in? Think about what it takes to tack a zero onto the very end of a number, and use only the primes in the fingerprint to figure it out. How many zeros does the number end in?
hints: A number ends in a zero exactly when it is divisible by \(10\). What two prime building blocks does \(10\) split into?
Since \(10 = 2 \times 5\), every trailing zero comes from one factor of \(2\) teamed up with one factor of \(5\). So count how many such teams you can make.
You have four \(2\)s and six \(5\)s. Pair them off one for one. The moment you run out of the scarcer prime, you cannot form any more complete \(2\)-and-\(5\) teams.
blocks.sol:
The secret is to ask what actually puts a zero on the end of a number. A number ends in a zero precisely when you can pull a factor of \(10\) out of it, and it ends in two zeros when you can pull out \(100 = 10 \times 10\), and so on. So the count of trailing zeros is really the count of how many factors of \(10\) are hiding inside the number.

Now look at \(10\) through the prime lens you have been building all chapter. It is not itself prime. It splits as

$$10 = 2 \times 5.$$

So every single factor of \(10\) is one factor of \(2\) shaking hands with one factor of \(5\). To make a trailing zero you need a matched pair, one \(2\) and one \(5\) together. Neither prime can make a zero alone.

The fingerprint tells you exactly how many of each prime you have on hand:

$$2^4 \times 3 \times 5^6 \quad\text{means four } 2\text{s and six } 5\text{s.}$$

Pair them up, one \(2\) with one \(5\) each time. You can form a first pair, a second, a third, and a fourth. But after the fourth pair your supply of \(2\)s is gone, while two lonely \(5\)s are left over with no partner. Those leftovers cannot make a zero by themselves. So you can build

$$10 \times 10 \times 10 \times 10 = 10^4,$$

and no more. The scarcer prime, the \(2\) with only four copies, sets the limit. The number ends in $\boxed{4}$ zeros.

Quick check. Pull those four \(10\)s out and see what is left behind. After removing \(2^4\) and \(5^4\), the leftover primes are \(5^2 \times 3 = 25 \times 3 = 75\), which ends in a \(5\), not a \(0\). That stray \(5\) confirms there is no fifth zero to be found, so four is exactly right.

The general idea is that the number of trailing zeros equals the number of \(2\)-and-\(5\) pairs you can form from the prime factorization, and the scarcer of those two primes decides how many you get.

blocks.x: A jeweler is designing a display and wants to know the smallest pile of gems that does several special things at once. First, every gem must fit into a perfect square arrangement on the velvet tray, with the same number of rows as columns and none left over. Second, the very same pile must be shareable with nothing left over into equal groups of \(2\), into equal groups of \(3\), into equal groups of \(5\), and into equal groups of \(7\). The jeweler does not want any pile that works, she wants the single smallest pile that satisfies all of these conditions together. What is the smallest number of gems she can use?
hints: Think in terms of the prime fingerprint of the number. For the pile to share evenly into groups of \(2\), of \(3\), of \(5\), and of \(7\), each of those primes has to appear in the fingerprint at least once.

Now bring in the perfect square idea from earlier. A number is a perfect square exactly when every exponent in its prime fingerprint is even. An odd count like one will not do, so each prime needs to show up an even number of times.

Combine the two requirements. Each of \(2, 3, 5, 7\) must appear, and each must appear an even number of times. The smallest even count that is still at least one is two, so try giving every prime an exponent of exactly \(2\) and compute the result.
blocks.sol:
Let us read the gem pile through its prime fingerprint, the way we have been doing all chapter. Every whole number has one unique fingerprint of primes, and that fingerprint tells us everything about how the number can be divided and arranged.

Start with the sharing conditions. For the pile to split evenly into groups of \(2\), the number must be divisible by \(2\), which means the prime \(2\) has to live somewhere in its fingerprint. The same reasoning forces \(3\), \(5\), and \(7\) into the fingerprint as well, since a prime can only divide a number if it appears in that number's fingerprint. So whatever the answer is, its fingerprint must contain at least one \(2\), one \(3\), one \(5\), and one \(7\).

Now add the square condition. From the earlier work, a number is a perfect square exactly when every exponent in its prime fingerprint is even, so the primes can pair off and one factor of each pair goes to the side length. A single copy of a prime is an odd count of one, which cannot pair up, so one copy of each prime is not enough. Each of \(2, 3, 5, 7\) must appear an even number of times.

Putting both ideas together, every one of these four primes must appear, and each must appear an even number of times. The smallest even number that is still at least one is \(2\), so the smallest pile gives each prime an exponent of exactly two.

$$2^2 \times 3^2 \times 5^2 \times 7^2 = (2 \times 3 \times 5 \times 7)^2 = 210^2 = 44100$$

So the smallest pile is \(\boxed{44100}\) gems.

Quick check. Since \(44100 = 210^2\), it is a perfect square with side \(210\). Its fingerprint is \(2^2 \times 3^2 \times 5^2 \times 7^2\), which clearly contains a \(2\), a \(3\), a \(5\), and a \(7\), so it divides evenly into groups of each of those sizes. Any smaller candidate would have to drop one of these primes or lower an exponent below two, which would break either a sharing rule or the even exponent rule.

The general idea is that when several divisibility demands meet the perfect square demand, you read off the required primes from the fingerprint and round each exponent up to the nearest even number, and the smallest number is built from exactly those minimal even powers.

blocks.x:
Here is something worth pausing over. The primes never run out. Suppose a very confident friend handed you a list and said it held every prime there is, the whole supply, finished and complete. You could prove them wrong with one clever move. Multiply together all the primes on their list, every single one, and then add 1 to the result. Call this new number \(N\). Now try dividing \(N\) by any prime on the list. Because that prime already went into the big product evenly, dividing \(N\) by it leaves a remainder of exactly 1 every time, so none of the listed primes divides \(N\) cleanly. But every whole number bigger than 1 has at least one prime factor, so \(N\) must have a prime factor hiding somewhere, and it cannot be any of the ones on the list. The supposedly complete list was missing a prime after all, which is impossible if it was truly complete. So no finite list can ever hold them all, and the primes march on forever.

Here is the companion surprise that makes the whole thing feel a little magical. Even though the primes go on without end, they thin out and leave gaps, and those gaps can be as wide as you please. Remember the stretch from 23 to 29 in this chapter, where 24, 25, 26, 27, and 28 are all composite and not a single prime appears. You can find a desert like that of any length you want, a run of a hundred or a thousand or a million consecutive whole numbers with no prime anywhere inside. So the primes are infinite in number, yet they are also generous with their empty spaces, scattered along the number line forever while still leaving room for silence as long as you like.

You now carry two tools out of this section, and they fit together. The first is a test for primeness. To decide whether a number is prime, you only have to try dividing by primes up to its square root, and your divisibility shortcuts from 3.2 sweep the small primes like 2, 3, and 5 away at a glance, so the check is usually quick. The second is the prime factorization itself, the unique fingerprint that every number above 1 has and no other number shares. Once you hold that fingerprint, a whole pile of questions answers itself. Is the number even, what does it divide by, is it a perfect square or a perfect cube, how many zeros does it end in, all of it is already written in the primes you found, with no extra work. So far you have studied one number at a time. The natural next move is to set two fingerprints side by side and read them together. The primes they both carry will point you to the greatest common divisor, and the primes they jointly demand will point you to the least common multiple, and that is exactly where 3.5 picks up. You have learned to break a number into its atoms. Next you get to see what happens when two numbers meet.

review.x: A librarian is reshelving books and wants to know which of five call numbers are prime. The numbers on the spines are 143, 149, 151, 161, and 167. She remembers a shortcut from section 3.1, that a number's factor pairs always meet around its square root, so to hunt for a hidden factor you only need to test primes up to the square root and no further. Every one of these five numbers is smaller than \(169 = 13^2\), so for each one it is enough to try dividing by the primes 2, 3, 5, 7, 11, and 13. If a number survives all of those with no clean division, it is prime. Walk through the five call numbers and decide which are prime. How many of the five are prime?
hints: All five are odd and none end in 0 or 5, so 2 and 5 will never divide them. That leaves only 3, 7, 11, and 13 to worry about.
Use the divisibility tests from section 3.2 to clear out 3 quickly, then test the survivors against 7, 11, and 13 by actual division.
Two of the five hide a factor. Try \(143 = 11 \times 13\) and look for a number that is \(7 \times 23\). The other three resist every prime up to 13.
review.sol: A number is prime when its only factors are 1 and itself, so the job is to look for any hidden factor. Here is the time saving idea from section 3.1. The factors of a number come in pairs that multiply to the number, and the two partners in a pair sit on opposite sides of the square root. So if a number had a factor larger than its square root, that factor would have a partner smaller than the square root, and we would have already found it. That means we never need to test past the square root. Since all five call numbers are below \(169 = 13^2\), their square roots are all under 13, and testing the primes 2, 3, 5, 7, 11, and 13 is enough. First clear the easy primes for all five. Every number here is odd, so 2 is out. None end in 0 or 5, so 5 is out. For 3, add the digits using the test from section 3.2. The digit sums are \(1+4+3=8\), \(1+4+9=14\), \(1+5+1=7\), \(1+6+1=8\), and \(1+6+7=14\), and none of those is a multiple of 3, so 3 divides none of them. That leaves only 7, 11, and 13 to check. Now test each survivor against 7, 11, and 13. $$143 = 11 \times 13 \quad\text{composite}$$ $$149 \div 7 = 21\text{ r }2,\quad 149 \div 11 = 13\text{ r }6,\quad 149 \div 13 = 11\text{ r }6 \quad\text{prime}$$ $$151 \div 7 = 21\text{ r }4,\quad 151 \div 11 = 13\text{ r }8,\quad 151 \div 13 = 11\text{ r }8 \quad\text{prime}$$ $$161 = 7 \times 23 \quad\text{composite}$$ $$167 \div 7 = 23\text{ r }6,\quad 167 \div 11 = 15\text{ r }2,\quad 167 \div 13 = 12\text{ r }11 \quad\text{prime}$$ So 143 and 161 are composite, while 149, 151, and 167 are prime. That gives \(\\boxed{3}\) primes among the five. Quick check. The two composite ones rebuild cleanly, since \(11 \\times 13 = 143\) and \(7 \\times 23 = 161\), and the three primes left a nonzero remainder against every prime up to 13, with nothing left to test because \(13^2 = 169\) already passes all five numbers. The general idea is that to decide whether a number is prime you only have to trial divide by the primes up to its square root, because any factor above the square root must already have a smaller partner below it.

review.x: A graphic designer is building a poster about prime numbers, and she wants the price tag in the corner to be a little puzzle. She decides the price should be a two-digit prime number, but with a twist. Both of its digits, the tens digit and the units digit, have to be digits that are themselves composite numbers. Remember that a composite number is a whole number bigger than \(1\) that has more factors than just \(1\) and itself. Among the single digits \(1\) through \(9\), some are prime, some are neither prime nor composite, and the rest are composite. The designer wants the largest possible price that fits her rule. What two-digit prime number, with both digits composite, is the largest one she can choose?
hints: First figure out which single digits actually count as composite. Check each digit from \(1\) to \(9\) and ask whether it has a factor other than \(1\) and itself. Only those digits are allowed in either spot.
You want the biggest number, so start as high as you can. Try a tens digit of \(9\) first, then \(8\), and for each one only allow units digits from your composite list.

When you test a candidate, use the divisibility ideas from section 3.2 to throw out numbers fast, then check the survivor against small primes like \(2\), \(3\), and \(7\) to confirm it really is prime.
review.sol:
First let us settle which single digits are composite, because that controls everything. A composite number has a factor beyond just \(1\) and itself. Checking the digits one at a time, \(1\) is special and counts as neither prime nor composite, while \(2\), \(3\), \(5\), \(7\) are prime, so none of those are allowed. That leaves
$$4 = 2 \times 2, \quad 6 = 2 \times 3, \quad 8 = 2 \times 4, \quad 9 = 3 \times 3,$$
so the composite digits are exactly \(4, 6, 8, 9\). Both the tens digit and the units digit of our number must come from this set.

Now we hunt for the largest two-digit prime built only from those digits. Since we want the biggest value, we scan downward starting from the largest possible tens digit. With a \(9\) in front, the candidates are \(99\), \(98\), \(96\), \(94\). But every one of these is even or a multiple of \(3\) or \(9\). Using the divisibility tests from section 3.2, \(94\), \(96\), \(98\) are all even, and \(99\) has digit sum \(18\) which is divisible by \(9\), so the whole group is composite. None survive.

So we drop to a tens digit of \(8\), and the largest units digit we can pair with it is \(9\), giving \(89\). Let us confirm \(89\) is prime. It is odd, so not divisible by \(2\). Its digit sum is \(8 + 9 = 17\), which is not a multiple of \(3\), so \(3\) does not divide it. It does not end in \(0\) or \(5\), so \(5\) is out. The only remaining prime to test is \(7\), since $$9^2 = 81 < 89 < 100 = 10^2,$$ meaning we only need primes up to \(9\). And \(7 \times 12 = 84\) while \(7 \times 13 = 91\), so \(7\) does not divide \(89\) either. With no prime up to its square root dividing it, \(89\) is prime, and both of its digits, \(8\) and \(9\), are composite.

So the largest two-digit prime with two composite digits is $$\boxed{89}.$$

Quick check: the digits \(8\) and \(9\) are both in our composite set \(4, 6, 8, 9\), and \(89\) passed every divisibility test up to its square root, so it really is a prime that fits the rule, and nothing in the \(90\)s could beat it.

The general idea is that when you want the largest thing meeting two conditions, you can scan from the top while quickly throwing out candidates with divisibility tests, and you only need to check primes up to the square root to be sure a number is prime.

review.x: At the Prime Pairs mixer, the host plays a matching game. Two guests get paired only if both of their ages are prime numbers and the two ages add up to exactly \(48\). For example, a \(7\)-year-old could pair with a \(41\)-year-old, since both \(7\) and \(41\) are prime and \(7 + 41 = 48\). The host wants to know every possible pairing of prime ages, counting a pair like (\(7, 41\)) the same as (\(41, 7\)) so nobody is counted twice. How many unordered pairs of prime numbers add up to \(48\)?
hints: Pick the smaller prime in each pair and walk it upward. For each small prime \(p\), the partner has to be \(48 - p\). You only need to check whether that partner is also prime.
Since \(48\) is even, two odd primes can add to it, so you can skip past \(2\) and try the odd primes \(3, 5, 7, 11, \dots\) as the smaller one.
To avoid counting a pair twice, stop once the smaller prime passes the halfway point \(24\). Any pair you would find beyond that you have already met from the other side.
review.sol:
The clean way to do this is to let the smaller prime in a pair climb upward, and for each one ask a single question, is its partner \(48 - p\) also prime. Because \(48\) is even, the only way two primes can add to it is if both are odd, since one even prime (which would have to be \(2\)) would force the partner to be \(46\), and \(46\) is not prime. So we walk through the odd primes as the smaller value.

Here is the walk, checking each partner \(48 - p\) for primality and stopping once \(p\) reaches the halfway point \(24\) so we never count a pair from both ends.

$$\begin{aligned} p = 3 &\rightarrow 48 - 3 = 45 = 5 \times 9 \quad \text{not prime} \\ p = 5 &\rightarrow 48 - 5 = 43 \quad \text{prime, pair } (5, 43) \\ p = 7 &\rightarrow 48 - 7 = 41 \quad \text{prime, pair } (7, 41) \\ p = 11 &\rightarrow 48 - 11 = 37 \quad \text{prime, pair } (11, 37) \\ p = 13 &\rightarrow 48 - 13 = 35 = 5 \times 7 \quad \text{not prime} \\ p = 17 &\rightarrow 48 - 17 = 31 \quad \text{prime, pair } (17, 31) \\ p = 19 &\rightarrow 48 - 19 = 29 \quad \text{prime, pair } (19, 29) \\ p = 23 &\rightarrow 48 - 23 = 25 = 5 \times 5 \quad \text{not prime} \end{aligned}$$

Once we pass \(24\), the smaller prime would actually be the larger one of a pair we already found, so we stop. Counting the successful rows gives the pairs (\(5, 43\)), (\(7, 41\)), (\(11, 37\)), (\(17, 31\)), and (\(19, 29\)), which is \(\\boxed{5}\) pairs.

Quick check. Add each pair back up. \(5 + 43 = 48\), \(7 + 41 = 48\), \(11 + 37 = 48\), \(17 + 31 = 48\), \(19 + 29 = 48\), and every number listed is prime. All five hold.

The general idea is that to find pairs of primes with a fixed even sum, you only need to march the smaller prime up to half the sum and test whether each partner is prime, which keeps the search short and never double counts.

review.x: A sticker printer fills one big sheet at a time, and each sheet holds at most 800 stickers. The machine only knows how to lay stickers down in 6 identical bundles, so the total on a sheet is always \(6 \times p\) where \(p\) is the bundle size. The shop owner has a quirky preference: she wants the bundle size \(p\) to be a prime number, because prime bundles are harder for a rival to copy by splitting evenly. To use as much of the sheet as possible without going over, she wants the largest prime \(p\) for which \(6 \times p\) still fits under the 800 limit. What is that prime bundle size \(p\)?
hints: First turn the packing limit into a ceiling on \(p\). If \(6 \times p\) must stay under 800, then \(p\) itself must stay under \(800 \div 6\). Work out that quotient before you go prime hunting.
The quotient \(800 \div 6\) is not a whole number, so \(p\) has to be at or below the largest whole number underneath it. Find that whole number first, then ask whether it is prime.

Once you have the top candidate, walk downward one number at a time and test each for primality using the divisibility tests from section 3.2. The first prime you land on is your answer. Watch out: a number ending in 1 or 3 can still be composite.
review.sol:
Start by translating the packing rule into a plain bound on \(p\). Since the sheet must hold \(6 \times p\) stickers and that count has to stay under 800, the bundle size cannot be too big. Divide the limit by the number of bundles to see exactly how big \(p\) is allowed to be.

$$ p < \frac{800}{6} = 133\tfrac{1}{3} $$

So \(p\) must be a whole number at or below \(133\tfrac{1}{3}\), which means \(p\) is at most 133. Now we hunt downward from 133 for the first value that is actually prime, since the owner insists on a prime bundle.

Check 133 first. It looks prime at a glance, but \(133 = 7 \times 19\), so it is composite and out. Step down to 132, which is even (it ends in 2), so it is divisible by 2 and cannot be prime. Step down once more to 131. Test it against the tools from section 3.2. It is odd, so 2 is out. Its digits \(1 + 3 + 1 = 5\) do not add to a multiple of 3, so 3 is out. It does not end in 0 or 5, so 5 is out. The next prime to try is 7, but \(7 \times 18 = 126\) and \(7 \times 19 = 133\), so 7 skips right over 131. Then 11 gives \(11 \times 11 = 121\) and \(11 \times 12 = 132\), again skipping past. Since \(12 \times 12 = 144\) is already above 131, we only needed to test primes up to about 11, and none divided in. So 131 is prime.

$$ p = \boxed{131} $$

Quick check: the full sheet would carry \(6 \times 131 = 786\) stickers, which is comfortably under 800, while the next prime up, 137, would need \(6 \times 137 = 822\) stickers and burst past the limit.

The general idea is that a "largest prime under a limit" question becomes easy once you convert the limit into a numeric ceiling and then walk downward, testing each candidate with divisibility tests until the first prime appears.

review.x: A puzzle club is making a poster with the title "Two Primes, One Sum." The challenge printed on it goes like this. Find two prime numbers that add up to \(39\). At first this feels like it could have many answers, since \(39\) is not very big and there are lots of small primes to try, like \(2\), \(3\), \(5\), \(7\), \(11\), and so on. But there is a hidden twist waiting for anyone who thinks about whether \(39\) is even or odd. It turns out there is exactly one pair of primes that works. What is the larger of those two primes?
hints: Is \(39\) even or odd? Now think about what happens when you add two odd numbers together, or two even numbers together.
To land on an odd total, one of your two primes has to be even and the other has to be odd. How many even prime numbers are there?
The only even prime is \(2\). If one prime must be \(2\), what is the other prime forced to be, and is that number actually prime?
review.sol:
Start by noticing the parity of the target. The number \(39\) is odd. Now think back to how parity behaves under addition. Two odd numbers add to an even number, and two even numbers also add to an even number. The only way to get an odd sum is to add one even number and one odd number. So in our pair of primes, one of them has to be even and the other has to be odd.

Here is the key squeeze. Among all the prime numbers, only one of them is even, and that is \(2\). Every other prime, like \(3\), \(5\), \(7\), \(11\), and beyond, is odd, because any larger even number is divisible by \(2\) and so cannot be prime. That means our even prime is forced to be \(2\), with no other choice.

Once one prime is pinned down as \(2\), the other is decided for us.

$$39 - 2 = 37$$

Now we just have to confirm that \(37\) really is prime. Following the idea from section 3.1, we only need to test factors up to the square root, since factor pairs cluster around \(\sqrt{37}\), which sits between \(6\) and \(7\). So we check \(2\), \(3\), and \(5\). Using the divisibility tests from section 3.2, \(37\) is odd so it is not divisible by \(2\), its digits sum to \(3 + 7 = 10\), which is not a multiple of \(3\), and it does not end in \(0\) or \(5\) so it is not divisible by \(5\). No factor up to \(6\) divides it, so \(37\) is prime. The one and only pair is \(2\) and \(37\), and the larger of the two is \(\boxed{37}\).

Quick check. Add them back together to be sure: \(2 + 37 = 39\), which is exactly the sum we wanted, and both \(2\) and \(37\) are prime.

The general idea is that when two primes add to an odd number, one of them must be \(2\), the lone even prime, which usually pins the whole pair down to a single possibility.

review.x: A cryptographer at QuanticaEdu is studying an old access code, and the code turns out to be the number 2310. She does not care how many times each prime shows up inside it. What she wants to know is how many *different* prime numbers you need to build 2310 by multiplication, because that count tells her how many secret "keys" the code was woven from. How many distinct prime factors does 2310 have?
hints: Start by stripping off the smallest primes one at a time. Is 2310 even? Does the digit-sum trick from 3.2 tell you about 3? Does it end in 0 or 5?
Each time you find a prime that divides cleanly, divide it out and keep working with the smaller number that is left. Keep going until what remains is itself a prime.
Count only the *different* primes you used. If a prime never repeated, you are simply listing the distinct primes whose product is 2310.
review.sol:
Let us peel 2310 apart one prime at a time, smallest first, using the divisibility shortcuts from 3.2.

It ends in 0, so it is even, and dividing by 2 gives \(2310 = 2 \times 1155\). Now look at 1155. Its digits add to \(1 + 1 + 5 + 5 = 12\), which is a multiple of 3, so 3 divides it and \(1155 = 3 \times 385\). The number 385 ends in 5, so 5 divides it and \(385 = 5 \times 77\). Finally \(77 = 7 \times 11\), and both 7 and 11 are prime, so we stop.

Stacking everything we pulled out gives

$$2310 = 2 \times 3 \times 5 \times 7 \times 11.$$

Notice that no prime repeated even once. The distinct primes are 2, 3, 5, 7, and 11, which is exactly the list of the first five prime numbers in order.

Quick check: multiply them back in pairs, \(2 \times 3 = 6\), \(6 \times 5 = 30\), \(30 \times 7 = 210\), and \(210 \times 11 = 2310\). It lands right back on 2310, so the factorization is correct.

So the number of distinct prime factors is

$$\boxed{5}.$$

The general idea is that you can find how many distinct primes build a number by peeling off the smallest prime that divides it again and again, and a number like 2310 that uses each small prime exactly once will have a distinct-prime count equal to how many primes you peeled.

review.x: Two tools, gathered. To decide whether a number is prime, divide it by the primes in order, \(2, 3, 5, 7, 11\) and onward, clearing the easy ones instantly with the divisibility shortcuts of the last section. You may stop the moment a prime climbs past the number's square root, since past that crossing any factor would have already named its smaller partner below the root, the very pairing you met for factor pairs. If no prime up to the root divides it, the number is prime. The second tool is deeper. Every whole number above \(1\) has exactly one prime fingerprint, a single product of primes that no other number shares. Read that fingerprint and a flood of questions answers itself at a glance. The number is even exactly when a \(2\) appears, and it is divisible by any number whose own primes all sit inside the fingerprint with enough copies. It is a perfect square exactly when every exponent is even, since a square needs each prime paired off, and a perfect cube exactly when every exponent is a multiple of \(3\). And it ends in as many zeros as it holds matched \(2\)-and-\(5\) pairs, since each \(2\times5\) builds one \(10\). One fingerprint, read once, settles them all.

A locked puzzle box flashes one equation on its screen, and you need the secret code to open it. The screen reads
$$2^x \times 3^y \times 5^z = 6000,$$
where \(x\), \(y\), and \(z\) are whole numbers. The box promises that exactly one set of values makes the equation true. Once you have found \(x\), \(y\), and \(z\), the code is their sum ( x + y + z ). What single number opens the box?
hints: The left side is already broken into prime building blocks. If you break 6000 into the same building blocks, the matching powers must line up. Try splitting 6000 into easier chunks first, like 6 and 1000.
Six is \(2 \times 3\), and 1000 is \(10 \times 10 \times 10\), so each thousand piece carries one \(2\) and one \(5\). Collect all the 2s together, all the 3s together, and all the 5s together.
Once 6000 is written purely as a power of 2 times a power of 3 times a power of 5, just read the three exponents straight off and add them.
review.sol:
The screen has done you a favor by writing the left side only in terms of the primes 2, 3, and 5. So the whole job is to write 6000 the same way and then match the powers.

Start by splitting 6000 into friendly chunks. A clean one is
$$6000 = 6 \times 1000.$$
Now break each chunk into primes. Six is \(2 \times 3\). A thousand is \(10 \times 10 \times 10\), and each ten is \(2 \times 5\), so \(1000 = 2^3 \times 5^3\). Putting the pieces together,
$$6000 = (2 \times 3) \times (2^3 \times 5^3) = 2^4 \times 3^1 \times 5^3.$$

Now line this up against the screen. The equation says \(2^x \times 3^y \times 5^z = 2^4 \times 3^1 \times 5^3\). Back in 3.1 you saw that a number's prime fingerprint is unique, so there is no second way to build 6000 from these primes. That uniqueness is what forces the exponents to agree one for one. The powers of 2 must match, the powers of 3 must match, and the powers of 5 must match, which gives \(x = 4\), \(y = 1\), and \(z = 3\). The code is their sum,
$$x + y + z = 4 + 1 + 3 = \boxed{8}.$$

Quick check. Rebuild the box's number from those exponents, \(2^4 \times 3 \times 5^3 = 16 \times 3 \times 125 = 48 \times 125 = 6000\). It lands exactly on 6000, so the exponents are right and the sum is 8.

The general idea is that once you write a number as a product of primes, the exponent on each prime is locked in, so matching prime powers on two sides of an equation forces the hidden exponents to be equal.

review.x: A tiler is laying out a square mosaic for a courtyard floor. She has exactly 252 identical square tiles in her cart, but when she tries to arrange them into a perfect square (the same number of rows as columns), she comes up short and ends up with leftovers no matter how she stacks them. So she decides to buy more of the same tile. She wants the new total to be a perfect square, meaning some whole number times itself, and to keep her receipt small she wants the new total to be as little above a clean multiple of her original batch as possible. To make the bookkeeping easy at the shop, she asks for her new total to be a whole-number multiple of 252. What is the smallest whole number she can multiply 252 by so that the result is a perfect square?
hints: Break 252 all the way down into a product of primes. Once it is written as primes raised to powers, the secret to being a perfect square lives in those exponents.
A whole number is a perfect square exactly when every prime in its factorization appears an even number of times. Look at your exponents on \(2\), \(3\), and \(7\). Which one is odd?
Only one prime has an odd exponent. You do not need to touch the others. Multiply by just enough copies of that one prime to make its exponent even.
review.sol:
Start by breaking 252 into primes, the same factoring move from 3.1. Pull out factors step by step. We get \(252 = 2 \times 126 = 2 \times 2 \times 63 = 2 \times 2 \times 7 \times 9\), and \(9 = 3 \times 3\), so

$$252 = 2^2 \times 3^2 \times 7.$$

Now think about what makes a number a perfect square. A perfect square is some whole number times itself, like \(42 \times 42\). When you square a number, every prime in it shows up twice as often, so a perfect square always has even exponents on all of its primes. Look at our three exponents. The 2 carries an exponent of 2, even. The 3 carries an exponent of 2, even. But the 7 carries an exponent of 1, odd, and that lone odd exponent is the only thing standing between 252 and a perfect square.

So we fix only what is broken. We do not waste a single factor on the 2 or the 3, since their exponents are already even. We just need one more 7 to turn \(7^1\) into \(7^2\). The smallest multiplier is therefore \(7\), and

$$252 \times 7 = 2^2 \times 3^2 \times 7^2 = (2 \times 3 \times 7)^2 = 42^2 = 1764.$$

Quick check. \(42 \times 42 = 1764\), and \(1764 \div 252 = 7\), so 1764 really is both a perfect square and a whole-number multiple of 252, using the smallest possible multiplier. Any smaller multiplier would leave the 7 with an odd exponent and could not produce a square.

The smallest multiplier is \(\boxed{7}\).

The general idea is that a number is a perfect square exactly when every exponent in its prime factorization is even, so to reach the nearest square you multiply only by the primes that currently sit at an odd exponent.

review.x: A sculptor keeps a bin of 72 identical wooden unit cubes and dreams of stacking them into one solid cube, the kind where the length, the width, and the height are all the same whole number. She lines them up and quickly sees that 72 is not a perfect cube, since \(4^3 = 64\) is too few and \(5^3 = 125\) is too many. So she decides to order more cubes from the same supplier, but the supplier only sells in matching batches, meaning she can buy 2 times, 3 times, or any whole number times her current 72. She wants the smallest whole number she can multiply 72 by so that the new total is a perfect cube. What is that smallest multiplier?
hints: Break 72 into its prime factorization first. Writing it as a product of primes raised to powers will let you see the structure clearly.

A number is a perfect cube exactly when every exponent in its prime factorization is a multiple of 3, since you are splitting the factors evenly into three identical groups. Check each exponent against the nearest multiple of 3.
The exponent on 2 is already fine. Look only at the exponent on 3 and ask how many more factors of 3 you must supply to push that exponent up to the next multiple of 3.
review.sol:
A perfect cube is a number you can write as \(n \times n \times n\) for some whole number \(n\), so its factors split evenly into three identical groups. That last phrase is the key. If you write the number using its prime factorization, then forming three identical groups means handing each group the same share of every prime, which is only possible when every exponent is a multiple of 3. So our whole job is to look at the exponents of 72 and nudge any that are not multiples of 3.

Start by factoring 72 the way you learned in 3.1, peeling off primes until only primes remain.

$$72 = 8 \times 9 = 2^3 \times 3^2$$

Now check each exponent against the nearest multiple of 3. The exponent on 2 is 3, which is already a multiple of 3, so the twos are perfectly balanced and need nothing. The exponent on 3 is 2, and the next multiple of 3 above 2 is 3 itself, so the threes are short by exactly one. To fix this without disturbing the twos, you multiply by a single extra 3.

$$72 \times 3 = 2^3 \times 3^3 = 6^3 = 216$$

Multiplying by anything smaller than 3 cannot work. Multiplying by 1 leaves 72, which we already saw is not a cube, and multiplying by 2 gives \(2^4 \times 3^2\), where now both exponents miss a multiple of 3. So 3 really is the smallest multiplier, giving \(\boxed{3}\).

Quick check: \(216 = 6 \times 6 \times 6\), and \(6^3 = 216\), so 216 is genuinely a perfect cube and her stack will be a tidy 6 by 6 by 6 block.

The general idea is that a number is a perfect cube exactly when every exponent in its prime factorization is a multiple of 3, so to reach the nearest cube you only need to top up the exponents that fall short.

review.x: Two whole numbers multiply to \(504\), and you happen to notice that each of them is divisible by \(6\). That alone would be a fun coincidence, but here is the twist. Neither of the two numbers is allowed to be \(6\) itself; each one is a genuine multiple of \(6\) that is bigger than \(6\). It turns out there is exactly one pair of whole numbers that fits all of this at once. What is the larger of the two numbers in that pair?
hints: Since each number is divisible by \(6\), try writing the first as \(6m\) and the second as \(6n\) for some whole numbers \(m\) and \(n\). What does their product look like now?
Pulling a \(6\) out of each factor gives \(6m \times 6n = 36 \times mn\). Set that equal to \(504\) and figure out what (mn) must be.
Once you know (mn), list the factor pairs of that small number. One pair forces a number to equal \(6\), which is forbidden, so cross it out and keep the pair that survives.
review.sol:
The phrase \"divisible by \(6\)\" is the key that unlocks everything, so let's lean on it from the very first line. If a number is divisible by \(6\), then it is \(6\) times some whole number. So write the first number as \(6m\) and the second as \(6n\), where \(m\) and \(n\) are whole numbers. Their product is

$$6m \times 6n = 36 \times mn.$$

We are told this product equals \(504\), so \(36 \\times mn = 504\). Dividing both sides by \(36\) gives

$$mn = \frac{504}{36} = 14.$$

A glance at the fingerprint of \(504\) confirms the division. Since \(504 = 2^3 \\times 3^2 \\times 7\) and \(36 = 2^2 \\times 3^2\), the two factors of \(36\) cancel cleanly and leave \(2 \\times 7 = 14\), exactly the \(14\) we found.

Now the problem shrinks to something tiny. We just need two whole numbers \(m\) and \(n\) that multiply to \(14\). The factor pairs of \(14\) are \(1 \\times 14\) and \(2 \\times 7\). Look at what each pair does to the original numbers. The pair \(1 \\times 14\) would make one of our numbers \(6 \\times 1 = 6\), and the rules forbid any number from equaling \(6\). So that pair is out. The surviving pair is \(2 \\times 7\), which gives the two original numbers

$$6 \times 2 = 12 \quad \text{and} \quad 6 \times 7 = 42.$$

The larger of the two is \(\\boxed{42}\).

Quick check. Both \(12\) and \(42\) are clearly multiples of \(6\), neither one is \(6\) itself, and \(12 \\times 42 = 504\). Everything holds.

The general idea is that when every factor shares a common building block, you can pull that block out of each one, turn a big product into a much smaller product, and solve the easy version that remains.

review.x: Maya is planning a rectangular community garden with an area of exactly 1271 square feet. The town's only rule is a quirky one. Both side lengths, measured in whole feet, have to be two-digit numbers, so each side is somewhere from 10 to 99 feet. Maya stares at the number 1271 and wonders whether such a rectangle even exists, and if it does, whether there might be several shapes to choose from. It turns out there is exactly one rectangle that works. What is the length, in feet, of its longer side?
hints: A side length of the rectangle must be a whole number that divides 1271 evenly, so you are really hunting for a factor of 1271.
From section 3.1, remember that factor pairs cluster around the square root. Since \(\sqrt{1271}\) is a little under 36, you only need to test factors up to about 35.
Use the divisibility tests from section 3.2 to skip the easy cases, then keep testing primes. Try 7, 11, 13, and onward. One of the primes in the low 30s divides 1271 cleanly.
review.sol:
Every rectangle with whole-number sides turns its area into a factor pair, so finding Maya's garden means finding two whole numbers that multiply to 1271. From section 3.1 you know factor pairs sit on either side of the square root, so the smaller factor of any pair is at most \(\\sqrt{1271}\). Since \(35^2 = 1225\) and \(36^2 = 1296\), the square root is between 35 and 36, which means you only need to test possible factors up to 35 and let each one hand you its partner.

Now walk up the primes and test them as divisors. The divisibility tests from section 3.2 clear out the small ones fast. The number 1271 is odd, so 2 is out. Its digits sum to \(1 + 2 + 7 + 1 = 11\), which is not a multiple of 3, so 3 is out. It does not end in 0 or 5, so 5 is out. Keep going by hand with 7, 11, 13, 17, 19, 23, 29, and none of them divide evenly. Then you reach 31 and strike gold.
$$1271 = 31 \times 41$$
So one side is 31 feet and its partner is \(1271 \\div 31 = 41\) feet. Both 31 and 41 are prime, meaning neither can be split into smaller whole-number factors bigger than 1, so there is no way to regroup this product into a different pair of whole numbers above 1. That is exactly why the rectangle is unique, and happily both 31 and 41 are two-digit numbers, so Maya's quirky rule is satisfied. The longer side is

$$\boxed{41}$$

Quick check. Multiply back to confirm the area: \(31 \\times 41 = 31 \\times 40 + 31 = 1240 + 31 = 1271\), exactly the area we wanted, and both 41 and 31 land in the 10 to 99 range.

The general idea is that when a number factors into two primes, that factorization is the only way to write it as a product of two whole numbers bigger than 1, so the rectangle it describes is one of a kind.

review.x: Mr. Okafor is decorating his classroom door with a number puzzle for arrival time. He wants to pin up three cards, each showing a different prime number, so that the three primes add up to exactly \(30\). A student named Priya stares at it and says, "Wait, before I start guessing, let me think about whether the numbers will end up even or odd." She has a clever point. Picture trying to use three odd primes like \(7\), \(11\), and a third odd prime. Add two odd numbers and you land on an even total, then add one more odd number and you swing back to an odd total, so three odd primes can never reach an even sum like \(30\). That forces a special prime into the mix. Once Priya figures out which prime is forced, the rest is just finding the right partners. How many different sets of three different primes add up to \(30\)?
hints: Think about even and odd, just like the parity reasoning from before. If all three primes were odd, what kind of total would you get, and does that match 30?
Since 30 is even but three odd primes give an odd sum, one of the primes has to be the only even prime there is. Which prime is that, and what must the other two add up to once you set it aside?

With one prime fixed as 2, you need two different primes that add to 28. Walk up from the smallest primes \(3, 5, 7, 11, ...\) and check which pairs of different primes total 28. Count how many such pairs you find.
review.sol:
Start where Priya started, with parity. Every prime except one is odd, and the lone exception is \(2\). If you tried to build \(30\) from three odd primes, the total would be odd, because adding three odd numbers always lands you on an odd result. But \(30\) is even, so three odd primes are impossible. That means one of your three primes is forced to be the even prime \(2\).

With \(2\) locked in, the other two primes must add up to what is left.
$$30 - 2 = 28$$
So now the whole puzzle shrinks to a single question. How many ways can two different primes add to \(28\)? Walk up from the small primes and pair each with its partner \(28 - p\), keeping only pairs where both numbers are prime and different.
$$28 = 5 + 23 \qquad 28 = 11 + 17$$
Checking the others rules them out. \(3 + 25\) fails because \(25 = 5 \times 5\) is not prime, \(7 + 21\) fails because \(21 = 3 \times 7\), and \(13 + 15\) fails because \(15 = 3 \times 5\). Past \(14\) the pairs just repeat what you already found. So exactly two pairs survive, giving the two sets \(\{2, 5, 23\}\) and \(\{2, 11, 17\}\).

Quick check. Add each set up: \(2 + 5 + 23 = 30\) and \(2 + 11 + 17 = 30\), and every number listed is prime and different from the others. Both genuinely work, so the count is

$$\boxed{2}$$

The general idea is that a parity argument can collapse a search before you ever start guessing, because spotting that an even target forces the prime \(2\) turns a three-number hunt into a quick two-number one.

review.x: Here is a mystery to carry with you. Almost three hundred years ago, a mathematician named Christian Goldbach noticed something while playing with even numbers, the same way you just split your numbers into pairs of primes. Every even number he tried, as long as it was bigger than 2, could be written as one prime plus another prime. Look at \(48 = 19 + 29\), or \(100 = 47 + 53\), and you can hunt down a pair for any even number you like. Goldbach guessed that this never stops being true, that every single even number all the way to infinity hides a pair of primes inside it. Today computers have checked this for even numbers so enormous you could not write one down if you filled every page of every book on Earth, and it has never failed, not even once. And yet here is the wonderful part. No person who has ever lived has been able to prove it must always work, for every even number without exception, and so it remains an open question called Goldbach's conjecture. When you were finding the two primes that add to a number in these problems, you were resting your hand on one of the oldest unsolved puzzles in all of mathematics, and nobody yet knows the ending.

blocks.x: Through this chapter we learned to take a single number apart, first into its factors, then into the unique prime fingerprint that lesson 3.3 gave us. Now we set two numbers next to each other and ask how they relate. Picture two lengths of ribbon, one 56 centimeters long and one 70, and a maker who wants to cut both into equal pieces with nothing wasted. How long can each piece be? That question lives entirely in what the two numbers share. Turn it around and ask a different question. Two lighthouse beacons flash on their own rhythms, and you want to know when they will next flash together. That one lives in what the two numbers jointly build. These are the two doors of this lesson. Behind the first waits the greatest common divisor, behind the second the least common multiple, and the prime fingerprints from 3.3 are the key that opens both at once.

A maker has two ribbons, one 56 centimeters long and one 70 centimeters long. She wants to snip both into pieces that are all exactly the same whole-centimeter length, using up every bit of ribbon with no scrap left over on either one. To waste the least effort she wants the pieces as long as possible. First list every piece length that divides 56 cleanly, then every length that divides 70 cleanly, and find the lengths that work for both ribbons. What is the longest piece length she can use?
hints: A piece length works for a ribbon only if it divides that ribbon's length with nothing left over. So write the divisors of 56 and the divisors of 70, then look for the numbers that show up on both lists.
The divisors of 56 are \(1, 2, 4, 7, 8, 14, 28, 56\) and the divisors of 70 are \(1, 2, 5, 7, 10, 14, 35, 70\). Which numbers sit on both lists, and which of those is biggest?
blocks.sol: A piece length can be used on a ribbon only when it divides that ribbon evenly, so the lengths that work for both ribbons are exactly the numbers that divide \(56\) and \(70\) at the same time. List each set of divisors and watch for the overlap. $$56: \quad 1,\ 2,\ 4,\ 7,\ 8,\ 14,\ 28,\ 56$$ $$70: \quad 1,\ 2,\ 5,\ 7,\ 10,\ 14,\ 35,\ 70$$ The numbers appearing on both lists are \(1, 2, 7,\) and \(14\). These are the common divisors, the only piece lengths that clear both ribbons with no scrap, and the largest of them is \(\boxed{14}\) centimeters. Quick check, \(56 = 14 \times 4\) and \(70 = 14 \times 5\), so the first ribbon gives four pieces and the second gives five, all of them \(14\) long and nothing left over. The general idea is that two numbers always share a small handful of common divisors, and the biggest one is the most a single piece can stretch while still fitting both.

blocks.x: The greatest common divisor of two whole numbers, written \(\gcd(a,b)\) and also called their greatest common factor, is the largest number that divides both of them with nothing left over. Every pair has at least \(1\) sitting on both divisor lists, so a common divisor always exists. And since lesson 3.1 showed that a number's divisors never climb past the number itself, each list is finite, the overlap is finite, and there is always a single greatest one. It is the most that two numbers can be cut down by at the same time.

Listing divisors is fine for small ribbons, but suppose the two numbers are \(168\) and \(180\), each with a long divisor list. Reach instead for the prime fingerprints from lesson 3.3, \(168 = 2^3 \times 3 \times 7\) and \(180 = 2^2 \times 3^2 \times 5\). A number that divides both can only be built from primes that both numbers actually carry, and it can use no more copies of a prime than the stingier of the two supplies. Use that to build the greatest common divisor straight from the fingerprints. What is \(\gcd(168, 180)\)?
hints: Go prime by prime. For the prime \(2\), the number \(168\) carries three of them and \(180\) carries two. A divisor of both can lean on at most the smaller count, two. Do the same for \(3\), and then ask whether \(5\) or \(7\) can appear at all.
A prime that lives in only one fingerprint cannot divide the other number, so it cannot appear in the gcd. Take each shared prime to the smaller of its two powers, then multiply those together.
blocks.sol: Set the two fingerprints side by side and read them prime by prime. $$168 = 2^3 \times 3^1 \times 7, \qquad 180 = 2^2 \times 3^2 \times 5$$ A common divisor is built only from primes both numbers own, using no more copies than the smaller supply allows, since you cannot borrow a factor a number does not have. For the prime \(2\), the smaller power is \(2^2\). For the prime \(3\), the smaller power is \(3^1\). The prime \(5\) lives only in \(180\) and the prime \(7\) lives only in \(168\), so neither can divide both and neither joins in. Multiplying the survivors, $$\gcd(168,180) = 2^2 \times 3 = \boxed{12}.$$ Quick check, \(168 = 12 \times 14\) and \(180 = 12 \times 15\), so \(12\) really does divide both, and nothing larger could, since any bigger common divisor would need a prime power neither fingerprint can spare. The general idea is that the gcd of two numbers is found by taking each shared prime to the lower of its two powers, which is far faster than listing every divisor.

blocks.x: Lay the two prime fingerprints side by side. The greatest common divisor takes each prime to the lower of its two powers, because a common divisor can only use primes both numbers carry, and only as many copies as the stingier number has. A prime that appears in just one fingerprint contributes nothing, since its lower power across the two is \(0\). When the two numbers share no prime at all, the only common divisor is \(1\), and we call such numbers coprime, or relatively prime. This is the very same coprime idea that lesson 3.2 needed for combining divisibility tests, now seen through the fingerprints.

Two interlocking gears mesh tooth to tooth. The small gear has \(24\) teeth and the large gear has \(35\) teeth, and a single tooth on each is painted red. The gears start with the two red teeth touching, then turn. The red teeth touch again only after a number of teeth has passed the meshing point that is a whole number of full turns for both gears at once, that is, a number divisible by \(24\) and by \(35\). First notice that \(24 = 2^3 \times 3\) and \(35 = 5 \times 7\) share no prime factor. Using that, how many teeth must pass the meshing point before the two red teeth touch again?
hints: The red teeth realign after a count of teeth that both \(24\) and \(35\) divide evenly, so you want the smallest number that is a multiple of both. Start listing multiples of \(35\) and check which is the first that \(24\) also divides.

Because \(24\) and \(35\) share no prime factor, a number divisible by both must carry all of \(24\)'s primes and all of \(35\)'s primes with no overlap to save on. The smallest such number is just \(24 \times 35\).
blocks.sol: The red teeth meet again at the first tooth count that is a whole number of turns for both gears, which means the smallest number divisible by \(24\) and by \(35\) at once. Look at the fingerprints, \(24 = 2^3 \times 3\) and \(35 = 5 \times 7\). They share nothing, so they are coprime. A number divisible by \(24\) must carry \(2^3\) and \(3\), and a number divisible by \(35\) must carry \(5\) and \(7\), and since none of these primes overlap, the smallest number holding all of them is simply their product. $$24 \times 35 = \boxed{840}$$ So \(840\) teeth pass the meshing point before the red teeth touch again. Quick check, \(840 = 24 \times 35\) gives the large gear \(24\) full turns and the small gear \(35\) full turns, both whole, so both red teeth are back at the meeting point together. The general idea is that when two numbers are coprime, nothing can be shared or saved, so the smallest number they both divide is just the two numbers multiplied together.

blocks.x: That last problem quietly opened the second door. Instead of the largest length that fits inside two numbers, we hunted for the smallest count that the two numbers fit evenly into, the first place their separate rhythms line up. The gears realigned at the smallest number that both \(24\) and \(35\) divide, and because they shared no factors, that number was the full product. But most pairs do share factors, and then the answer is smaller than the product, because the shared part does not need to be counted twice. To see exactly how much smaller, we go back to listing, then back to the fingerprints.

Two beacons sweep the harbor. The first flashes every \(9\) seconds, at seconds \(9, 18, 27,\) and onward, and the second flashes every \(12\) seconds, at \(12, 24, 36,\) and onward. They both flash at second \(0\) to begin. List the flash times of each beacon and find the first moment after the start when both flash at the very same second. At what second do they next flash together?
hints: The first beacon flashes at the multiples of \(9\), the second at the multiples of \(12\). You want the first number that shows up on both lists of multiples.
Multiples of \(9\) are \(9, 18, 27, 36, 45, \ldots\) and multiples of \(12\) are \(12, 24, 36, 48, \ldots\). What is the smallest number appearing on both?
blocks.sol: The first beacon flashes at the multiples of \(9\) and the second at the multiples of \(12\), so they flash together exactly at the numbers that are multiples of both. Walk the two lists until they first meet. $$9: \quad 9,\ 18,\ 27,\ 36,\ 45,\ 54,\ \ldots$$ $$12: \quad 12,\ 24,\ 36,\ 48,\ \ldots$$ The first number on both lists is \(36\), so the beacons next flash together at second \(\boxed{36}\). Quick check, \(36 = 9 \times 4\) and \(36 = 12 \times 3\), so the first beacon has flashed four times and the second three times, both landing on second \(36\). Notice this is smaller than \(9 \times 12 = 108\), because \(9\) and \(12\) share a factor of \(3\) that does not need to be counted twice. The general idea is that two repeating events coincide at the common multiples of their periods, and the first coincidence is the smallest of those.

blocks.x: The least common multiple of two whole numbers, written \(\operatorname{lcm}(a,b)\), is the smallest positive number that both of them divide evenly, the smallest number that is a multiple of each. Two events that repeat every \(a\) and every \(b\) steps line up exactly at the common multiples of \(a\) and \(b\), and the first time they line up is the least common multiple. You might wonder why we never speak of a greatest common multiple. It is because multiples run on forever, as lesson 3.1 showed, so there is no largest common multiple to find. The least one is the only interesting extreme.

Return to \(168\) and \(180\), with fingerprints \(168 = 2^3 \times 3 \times 7\) and \(180 = 2^2 \times 3^2 \times 5\). Now hunt for the smallest number that both divide, their least common multiple. A number divisible by \(168\) must carry enough copies of every prime in \(168\), and a number divisible by \(180\) must carry enough for every prime in \(180\), so the lcm has to satisfy the hungrier demand for each prime. Build it from the fingerprints. What is \(\operatorname{lcm}(168, 180)\)?
hints: Go prime by prime again, but this time take the larger demand. For \(2\), the number \(168\) wants \(2^3\) while \(180\) only wants \(2^2\), so the lcm must supply \(2^3\) to satisfy \(168\). Do the same for every prime that appears in either number.
Every prime in either fingerprint must appear in the lcm, each to the highest power demanded by either number. Collect \(2^3\), \(3^2\), \(5\), and \(7\), then multiply.
blocks.sol: A number that both \(168\) and \(180\) divide must contain enough of every prime to satisfy each of them, so for each prime it must meet the larger of the two demands. $$168 = 2^3 \times 3^1 \times 7, \qquad 180 = 2^2 \times 3^2 \times 5$$ For \(2\), the larger power is \(2^3\), demanded by \(168\). For \(3\), the larger power is \(3^2\), demanded by \(180\). The prime \(5\) is demanded by \(180\) and the prime \(7\) by \(168\), so both must appear. Multiplying these highest demands together, $$\operatorname{lcm}(168,180) = 2^3 \times 3^2 \times 5 \times 7 = 8 \times 9 \times 5 \times 7 = \boxed{2520}.$$ Quick check, \(2520 = 168 \times 15\) and \(2520 = 180 \times 14\), both whole, so \(2520\) really is a multiple of each, and nothing smaller could be, since dropping any prime power would starve one of the two numbers. The general idea is that the lcm of two numbers takes each prime to the higher of its two powers, the mirror image of how the gcd takes the lower.

blocks.x: Lay the two fingerprints side by side once more. The least common multiple takes each prime to the higher of its two powers, because a common multiple must hold enough copies of every prime to be divisible by each number, and the hungrier number sets the bar. Every prime that appears in either fingerprint shows up in the lcm, at its highest power, even a prime that only one of the numbers carries. So the gcd and the lcm are two readings of the very same pair of fingerprints, one taking the low power of each shared prime, the other taking the high power of every prime in sight.

<svg viewBox="0 0 400 256" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" role="img" aria-label="Two overlapping circles, one for the prime factors of 168 and one for 180. The shared overlap multiplies to the greatest common divisor 12, and everything in both circles together multiplies to the least common multiple 2520.">
